pantheons.name: Proto-Indo-European
pantheons.description: Reconstructed religion of the speakers of Proto-Indo-European, ancestor of many later pantheons (Greek, Roman, Vedic, Norse, Slavic, Celtic). Reconstructed from comparative mythology.

pantheons.name: Greek
pantheons.description: Olympian pantheon of ancient Greece, systematized by Hesiod and Homer. The most genealogically documented of ancient religions.

pantheons.name: Roman
pantheons.description: Roman state religion, heavily syncretized with Greek deities via interpretatio graeca while retaining native Italic figures.

pantheons.name: Egyptian
pantheons.description: Ancient Egyptian religion, with regional variants centered on Heliopolis (Ennead), Memphis, and Thebes. Shifted between monolatry and polytheism.

pantheons.name: Mesopotamian
pantheons.description: Composite tradition of Sumer, Akkad, Babylon, and Assyria. Underwent syncretism across three millennia; one of the earliest written religions.

pantheons.name: Canaanite / Phoenician
pantheons.description: Semitic pantheon of the Levant, preserved most fully in the Ugaritic texts. Heavily influenced early Israelite religion.

pantheons.name: Hittite
pantheons.description: Bronze Age Anatolian religion, famously the 'thousand gods of Hatti' — a syncretic mix of Hattic, Hurrian, and Indo-European layers.

pantheons.name: Norse
pantheons.description: Germanic / Scandinavian religion, preserved mainly in the Icelandic Eddas. Divided into Aesir and Vanir tribes.

pantheons.name: Celtic (Irish)
pantheons.description: Gaelic polytheism, centered on the Tuatha Dé Danann. Preserved in Christian-era Irish manuscripts.

pantheons.name: Celtic (Welsh)
pantheons.description: Brythonic mythology preserved in medieval Welsh texts (the Mabinogi). Many figures overlap with Irish Celtic and later Arthurian tradition.

pantheons.name: Slavic
pantheons.description: Pre-Christian Slavic religion. Sparsely attested due to late Christianization and minimal pagan-era literacy.

pantheons.name: Finno-Ugric
pantheons.description: Finnish and Karelian religion, preserved in oral poetry compiled as the Kalevala. Shamanic, with strong nature-spirit emphasis.

pantheons.name: Etruscan
pantheons.description: Non-Indo-European religion of pre-Roman Italy. Influenced Roman religion (especially divination) before being absorbed.

pantheons.name: Zoroastrian
pantheons.description: Iranian religion founded by Zarathustra. Dualistic: the good Ahura Mazda vs. the evil Angra Mainyu. Still practiced by Parsis today.

pantheons.name: Hindu / Vedic
pantheons.description: Vedic religion evolving into classical Hinduism. One of the oldest continuously practiced traditions. Trimurti (Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva) + avatars + countless regional deities.

pantheons.name: Chinese
pantheons.description: Composite Chinese folk religion blending Taoist, Confucian, and Buddhist elements. Includes heavenly bureaucracy, ancestor veneration, and deified historical figures.

pantheons.name: Shinto
pantheons.description: Japanese indigenous religion of kami — spirits of nature, ancestors, and places. Synthesized with Buddhism through most of its history.

pantheons.name: Korean
pantheons.description: Korean folk religion (Muism) centered on shamans (mudang) and a pantheon often organized around the Okhwang Sangje (Jade Emperor of Heaven).

pantheons.name: Aztec / Mexica
pantheons.description: Religion of the Mexica and related Nahua peoples. Built on earlier Mesoamerican layers (Olmec, Toltec). Complex calendar and ritual system.

pantheons.name: Mayan
pantheons.description: Religion of the Maya city-states across Yucatán, Guatemala, and Belize. Preserved in the Popol Vuh and surviving codices.

pantheons.name: Inca
pantheons.description: State religion of the Inca Empire, centered on sun-worship (Inti) and ancestor veneration. Built on earlier Andean religions.

pantheons.name: Yoruba
pantheons.description: West African religion of the Yoruba people. Orishas (deities) numbered at 401 or 'irinwo'. Survives in Americas as Santería, Candomblé, and Vodou.

pantheons.name: Polynesian
pantheons.description: Shared pantheon of Polynesian cultures (Hawaiian, Maori, Tahitian, Samoan). Core deities with regional variants across the Pacific.

pantheons.name: Judaism
pantheons.description: Strictly monotheistic Abrahamic religion. 'Divine beings' here catalog YHWH, angels, and demonic figures from Tanakh and later tradition — not additional gods.

pantheons.name: Christianity
pantheons.description: Monotheistic Abrahamic religion built on Jewish foundations. Catalogs Trinity, angels, Mary, and demonic figures — understood within Christianity as one God, not a pantheon.

pantheons.name: Islam
pantheons.description: Strictly monotheistic Abrahamic religion. Catalogs Allah, angels, jinn, and prophets — not additional deities. Included here for cross-cultural comparison of divine-being categories.

pantheons.name: Baltic
pantheons.description: Lithuanian, Latvian, and Old Prussian religion — the last European pagan tradition to be Christianized (Lithuania, 1387). Preserved through folklore and the Latvian dainas.

pantheons.name: Armenian
pantheons.description: Pre-Christian Armenian religion. Syncretic — combined Indo-European, Iranian (Zoroastrian), and Mesopotamian layers. Armenia was the first state to adopt Christianity (301 CE).

pantheons.name: Ainu
pantheons.description: Indigenous religion of the Ainu. Animistic; kamuy (spirits) inhabit animals, plants, and objects. The bear ceremony (iomante) is central.

pantheons.name: Tibetan Bon
pantheons.description: Pre-Buddhist Tibetan religion. Now heavily syncretized with Buddhism but retains distinct deities, rituals, and its own founder (Tonpa Shenrab).

pantheons.name: Tengrism
pantheons.description: Religion of the Eurasian steppe — Turks, Mongols, Huns, Xiongnu. Centered on Tengri, the eternal blue sky. State religion of several steppe empires including the early Mongol Empire.

pantheons.name: Vietnamese
pantheons.description: Vietnamese folk religion. Blends indigenous mother-goddess worship (Đạo Mẫu) and ancestor-veneration with Taoist, Confucian, and Buddhist elements.

pantheons.name: Pre-Islamic Arabian
pantheons.description: Pre-Islamic Arabian polytheism. The Kaaba originally housed 360 idols. Allah was one among many high-gods before Islam's radical monotheism.

pantheons.name: Lakota
pantheons.description: Religion of the Lakota peoples. Centered on Wakan Tanka (the 'Great Mystery') and the seven sacred rites given by White Buffalo Calf Woman.

pantheons.name: Diné (Navajo)
pantheons.description: Religion of the Diné (Navajo) people. Centered on Changing Woman, her Hero Twins, and the Diyin Diné (Holy People).

pantheons.name: Haudenosaunee
pantheons.description: Religion of the Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) Confederacy. Sky Woman's descent; the dual creators Sapling and Flint; central to the Longhouse tradition.

pantheons.name: Fon / Dahomey
pantheons.description: Religion of the Fon. Organized pantheon of Vodun (spirits). Via the Atlantic slave trade, parent tradition of Haitian Vodou and Louisiana Voodoo.

pantheons.name: Zulu
pantheons.description: Zulu religion. Unkulunkulu is first-human and creator; ancestor-veneration (amadlozi) is central to daily practice.

pantheons.name: Guaraní
pantheons.description: Religion of the Guaraní peoples. Centered on Ñamandú, the primordial creator. Cosmology of Yvy Marã Ey, the 'Land Without Evil'.

pantheons.name: Mapuche
pantheons.description: Religion of the Mapuche. Ngenechen rules the Wenumapu (upper world); dualistic opposition with the destructive Wekufe.

pantheons.name: Gnostic
pantheons.description: Dualist syncretic traditions of the early Roman Empire. True God (the Monad) opposed to the Demiurge; aeons emanate from the Pleroma. Suppressed as heretical, but influenced later dualisms (Manichaeism, Cathars).

pantheons.name: Kabbalistic
pantheons.description: Jewish mystical tradition mapping the divine as ten Sefirot — emanations between the hidden Ein Sof and creation. Not a separate religion; an esoteric layer of Judaism.

pantheons.name: Aboriginal Australian
pantheons.description: Religions of the Aboriginal Australians — the oldest continuous living traditions on Earth. Hundreds of distinct Dreamings (Altjeringa) belong to different language groups; a few figures are widely shared.

pantheons.name: Taíno
pantheons.description: Religion of the Taíno peoples of the Greater Antilles. Systematically destroyed within 50 years of European contact; reconstructed from early chroniclers (Pané, Las Casas).

pantheons.name: Haitian Vodou
pantheons.description: Syncretic religion of Haiti. Fon/Dahomean and Kongo roots fused with Catholic saints under slavery. Lwa (spirits) are grouped into 'nations' (Rada, Petwo, Gede).

pantheons.name: Sámi
pantheons.description: Indigenous religion of the Sámi people of northern Scandinavia. Shamanic; noaidi drummed visions. Suppressed by Lutheran missionaries from the 17th century but partially preserved.

pantheons.name: Thracian
pantheons.description: Religion of the Thracians (and related Dacians and Getae). Influenced Greek mystery religions via cults of Sabazios and Dionysus. Zalmoxis promised immortality to his followers.

pantheons.name: Inuit
pantheons.description: Religion of the Inuit peoples. Animistic — animals and landscapes have inua (spirits). Shamans (angakkuit) travel to the sea-mother Sedna to ensure hunting success.

pantheons.name: Akan
pantheons.description: Religion of the Akan peoples (Ashanti, Fante, and others). Supreme god Nyame is remote; lesser abosom handle most affairs. Ancestor spirits (nsamanfo) are actively involved in family life.

pantheons.name: Tagalog
pantheons.description: Pre-Hispanic Tagalog religion of Luzon. Supreme Bathala ruled a pantheon of anito (spirits) and abstract forces. Largely suppressed by Spanish Catholicism from 1565 onward.

pantheons.name: Manichaean
pantheons.description: Dualist religion founded by Mani (216–276 CE). At its peak rivalled Christianity; stretched from the Atlantic to China. Ruthlessly suppressed; the last community (Cathars in France, Uyghurs in Central Asia) vanished by ~1400.

pantheons.name: Pacific Northwest
pantheons.description: Religion of the Pacific Northwest peoples. Raven is the great trickster-culture-hero. Totemic animals have supernatural counterparts; the Thunderbird is cosmic guardian.

pantheons.name: Sikh
pantheons.description: Founded by Guru Nanak (1469–1539). Monotheistic — one formless Ik Onkar. The ten human Gurus transmitted the teaching; since 1708 the Guru Granth Sahib (scripture) is the eternal Guru. Figures listed here are historical Gurus, not deities.

pantheons.name: Rastafari
pantheons.description: Religion emerging from 1930s Jamaica. Identifies Haile Selassie I of Ethiopia (crowned 1930) as the Second Coming of Christ / manifestation of Jah. Afrocentric; influential on reggae and Caribbean political thought.

pantheons.name: Bahá'í
pantheons.description: Monotheistic religion founded by Bahá'u'lláh (1817–1892). Central figures here are 'Manifestations of God', not deities — but are treated as divine mediators by Bahá'ís. Now practiced in over 200 countries.

pantheons.name: Cao Đài
pantheons.description: Syncretic Vietnamese religion founded 1926. Fuses Buddhism, Taoism, Confucianism, Christianity, and Islam under a single Supreme Being. At one point the third-largest religion in Vietnam.

pantheons.name: Deified Humans
pantheons.description: Historical persons posthumously (or sometimes during life) elevated to divine status. Includes Roman emperors, Chinese folk saints, Shinto kami, and Hellenistic ruler cults.

archetypes.name: Sky Father
archetypes.description: Chief sky/thunder god, often king of the pantheon. The most-reconstructed PIE deity (*Dyēus Phter → Zeus, Jupiter, Dyaus Pita).

archetypes.name: Earth Mother
archetypes.description: Fertility / earth goddess, often consort of the sky father. Nourisher and grave.

archetypes.name: Storm / Thunder God
archetypes.description: Warrior god of thunder and rain, often distinct from sky-father in younger pantheons. Serpent-slayer motif is near-universal.

archetypes.name: Sun Deity
archetypes.description: Personification of the sun; variable gender across cultures.

archetypes.name: Moon Deity
archetypes.description: Personification of the moon; gender varies — female in many Indo-European traditions, male in Mesopotamian and Japanese.

archetypes.name: Great Mother
archetypes.description: Creator / mother / fertility goddess distinct from earth. Often the primordial feminine.

archetypes.name: Goddess of Love
archetypes.description: Goddess of love, sexuality, and beauty. Often tied to planet Venus.

archetypes.name: Trickster
archetypes.description: Liminal figure who breaks rules, often brings culture to humanity but causes chaos.

archetypes.name: Ruler of the Dead
archetypes.description: God who rules the underworld or realm of the dead.

archetypes.name: War God
archetypes.description: Deity of warfare, battle-frenzy, or strategic combat.

archetypes.name: Smith God
archetypes.description: Divine craftsman, often lame or injured.

archetypes.name: Creator / Demiurge
archetypes.description: Primary creator of the cosmos. May be remote ('otiose') in later mythology.

archetypes.name: Dying-and-Rising God
archetypes.description: Deity who dies and returns, often tied to seasonal vegetation cycles.

archetypes.name: Psychopomp
archetypes.description: Guide of souls to the afterlife.

archetypes.name: Wisdom / Writing
archetypes.description: Deity of knowledge, wisdom, scribes, or writing.

archetypes.name: Serpent / Chaos-Dragon
archetypes.description: Primordial serpent or dragon, often slain by the storm-god to establish cosmic order (chaoskampf).

gods.name: Dyēus Ph₂tḗr
gods.description: Reconstructed chief PIE sky-god; the etymological root of Zeus, Jupiter, Dyaus Pitar, Tyr, and the Latin word 'deus'.

gods.name: Dʰéǵʰōm
gods.description: Reconstructed PIE earth-mother, consort of Dyēus. Source of Greek Khthōn, Slavic Zemlya, Lithuanian Zemyna.

gods.name: H₂éwsōs
gods.description: Reconstructed PIE dawn goddess. Continued as Eos, Aurora, Ushas, and (likely) Eostre/Ostara.

gods.name: Diwós Sunū
gods.description: Reconstructed PIE divine twins, 'sons of Dyēus'. Became the Dioscuri (Castor & Pollux), Vedic Ashvins, Germanic Alcis.

gods.name: Perkʷunos
gods.description: Reconstructed PIE thunder god. Ancestor of Slavic Perun, Baltic Perkunas, Vedic Parjanya, Germanic Fjörgynn.

gods.name: Trito
gods.description: Reconstructed first warrior-hero who slays a three-headed serpent (Ngʷhi) to recover stolen cattle. Prototype of Indra-Vritra, Thor-Jörmungandr, Heracles-Geryon.

gods.name: Chaos
gods.description: The primordial void from which the first gods emerged in Hesiod's Theogony.

gods.name: Gaia
gods.description: Primordial Earth, mother of the Titans and most subsequent divine life. Goaded her children to castrate Uranus.

gods.name: Uranus
gods.description: Primordial sky, son-husband of Gaia. Castrated by his son Cronus; from his severed genitals came Aphrodite.

gods.name: Tartarus
gods.description: The abyss below Hades, prison of the Titans. Both a place and a primordial being.

gods.name: Eros (Primordial)
gods.description: Primordial generative desire. Distinct from the later Eros, son of Aphrodite.

gods.name: Erebus
gods.description: Primordial darkness of the underworld.

gods.name: Nyx
gods.description: Primordial night. Feared even by Zeus.

gods.name: Pontus
gods.description: Primordial sea, preceding Poseidon.

gods.name: Cronus
gods.description: Youngest Titan; overthrew Uranus and ruled the Golden Age. Swallowed his children to prevent being overthrown, until Zeus escaped.

gods.name: Rhea
gods.description: Titaness mother of the Olympians. Hid infant Zeus from Cronus by giving him a swaddled stone to swallow.

gods.name: Oceanus
gods.description: Titan of the great river encircling the world. Father of all rivers and three thousand Oceanid nymphs.

gods.name: Tethys
gods.description: Titaness of fresh water and nursing. With Oceanus, mother of all the world's rivers and oceanids.

gods.name: Hyperion
gods.description: Titan of heavenly light. Father of Sun, Moon, and Dawn.

gods.name: Theia
gods.description: Titaness of sight and the shining ether. Consort of Hyperion.

gods.name: Coeus
gods.description: Titan of the inquiring mind. Grandfather of Apollo and Artemis.

gods.name: Phoebe
gods.description: Titaness of prophecy, original owner of the Delphic oracle.

gods.name: Iapetus
gods.description: Titan; father of Prometheus, Atlas, and Epimetheus — ancestor of humanity in some accounts.

gods.name: Themis
gods.description: Titaness of divine law and natural order. Early consort of Zeus; held Delphi before Apollo.

gods.name: Mnemosyne
gods.description: Titaness of memory; mother of the nine Muses by Zeus.

gods.name: Crius
gods.description: Least-individuated of the male Titans; father of Astraeus, Pallas, and Perses.

gods.name: Prometheus
gods.description: Titan who stole fire from the gods for humanity; punished by eternal torture. Sometimes credited with creating humans from clay.

gods.name: Epimetheus
gods.description: 'Afterthought', brother of Prometheus. Married Pandora, bringing evil into the world.

gods.name: Atlas
gods.description: Titan condemned to hold up the sky after the Titanomachy.

gods.name: Leto
gods.description: Titaness mother of Apollo and Artemis, conceived during an affair with Zeus; persecuted across the world by a jealous Hera.

gods.name: Zeus
gods.description: King of the Olympian gods. Led the overthrow of Cronus. Swallowed Metis to prevent a prophesied overthrow, then birthed Athena from his head.

gods.name: Hera
gods.description: Queen of the gods; sister and wife of Zeus. Relentlessly persecutes Zeus's lovers and illegitimate children.

gods.name: Poseidon
gods.description: God of the sea, earthquakes, and horses. Brother of Zeus, ruler of the second kingdom.

gods.name: Hades
gods.description: God of the underworld and the dead. Abducted Persephone as his queen.

gods.name: Demeter
gods.description: Goddess of grain and the harvest. Her grief over Persephone's abduction explains the seasons.

gods.name: Hestia
gods.description: Virgin goddess of the hearth. Voluntarily gave up her Olympian seat to Dionysus.

gods.name: Athena
gods.description: Virgin goddess of wisdom and strategic warfare. Sprang fully-armed from Zeus's head after he swallowed her pregnant mother Metis.

gods.name: Apollo
gods.description: God of music, prophecy, and (later) the sun. Twin of Artemis. Slew the Python at Delphi.

gods.name: Artemis
gods.description: Virgin goddess of the hunt and wild places. Twin of Apollo. Protector of young women; ruthless to transgressors.

gods.name: Ares
gods.description: God of warfare, bloodlust, and martial rage. Unloved by his parents.

gods.name: Aphrodite
gods.description: Goddess of love and beauty. Born from sea-foam when Uranus's castrated genitals fell into the sea. Unhappily married to Hephaestus.

gods.name: Hephaestus
gods.description: Lame smith of the gods. Cast from Olympus by Hera for his deformity. Forged the gods' weapons and palaces.

gods.name: Hermes
gods.description: Messenger of the gods, psychopomp, patron of thieves and travelers. Stole Apollo's cattle on his first day.

gods.name: Dionysus
gods.description: Twice-born god of wine and ecstatic release. Born from Zeus's thigh after Semele was destroyed. Later replaced Hestia on Olympus.

gods.name: Helios
gods.description: Personification of the sun. Drove a fiery chariot across the sky each day. Often conflated with Apollo in later tradition.

gods.name: Selene
gods.description: Personification of the moon. Loved the mortal Endymion, granted eternal sleep so she could visit him nightly.

gods.name: Eos
gods.description: Rosy-fingered dawn. Begged Zeus for Tithonus's immortality but forgot to ask for eternal youth.

gods.name: Persephone
gods.description: Queen of the underworld. Abducted by Hades; spends half the year below (winter) and half above.

gods.name: Hecate
gods.description: Titaness-goddess of magic and crossroads. Held honors in heaven, earth, and sea; torch-bearing companion to Persephone.

gods.name: Pan
gods.description: Goat-legged god of shepherds, wild places, and panic.

gods.name: Eros
gods.description: God of romantic desire. Armed with arrows that compel love. Married the mortal Psyche after many trials.

gods.name: Nike
gods.description: Winged goddess of victory. Companion of Zeus and Athena.

gods.name: Iris
gods.description: Rainbow-messenger of the gods, especially Hera.

gods.name: Asclepius
gods.description: Mortal-born god of medicine. Zeus struck him down for resurrecting the dead; later deified.

gods.name: The Nine Muses
gods.description: Nine goddesses of the arts: Calliope, Clio, Erato, Euterpe, Melpomene, Polyhymnia, Terpsichore, Thalia, Urania.

gods.name: Moirai (Fates)
gods.description: Clotho (spinner), Lachesis (allotter), Atropos (cutter). Three sisters who spin, measure, and cut the thread of each mortal life.

gods.name: Nemesis
gods.description: Goddess of divine retribution against hubris.

gods.name: Thanatos
gods.description: Personification of peaceful death; twin of Hypnos (sleep).

gods.name: Hypnos
gods.description: Personification of sleep; twin of Thanatos.

gods.name: Charon
gods.description: Ferryman of the dead across the Styx.

gods.name: Typhon
gods.description: Hundred-headed monstrous son of Gaia. Challenged Zeus for kingship; defeated and pinned under Mount Etna.

gods.name: Echidna
gods.description: Half-woman, half-serpent; mother of most Greek monsters.

gods.name: Heracles
gods.description: Greatest Greek hero; completed twelve impossible labors. Deified after his death, married Hebe on Olympus.

gods.name: Perseus
gods.description: Slayer of Medusa; grandfather of Heracles.

gods.name: Theseus
gods.description: Athenian founder-king who slew the Minotaur.

gods.name: Jupiter
gods.description: Roman chief god, equated with Zeus; head of the Capitoline Triad (Jupiter, Juno, Minerva).

gods.name: Juno
gods.description: Queen of the Roman gods; protector of Rome's women and state.

gods.name: Neptune
gods.description: Roman god of the sea, equated with Poseidon; originally a fresh-water deity.

gods.name: Pluto
gods.description: Roman god of the underworld; emphasizes the 'rich one' aspect of Hades.

gods.name: Saturn
gods.description: Pre-Olympian king of a Golden Age in Italy; conflated with Cronus. The Saturnalia festival reversed social order in his honor.

gods.name: Ops
gods.description: Roman goddess of agricultural abundance; consort of Saturn.

gods.name: Ceres
gods.description: Roman goddess of grain and agriculture, source of the word 'cereal'.

gods.name: Vesta
gods.description: Virgin goddess of the hearth; her sacred flame was tended by the Vestal Virgins.

gods.name: Mars
gods.description: Roman war-god, second only to Jupiter. Father of Romulus and Remus. Originally also an agricultural deity.

gods.name: Venus
gods.description: Roman love-goddess; ancestral mother of the Roman people through Aeneas.

gods.name: Vulcan
gods.description: Roman smith-god of fire and volcanoes.

gods.name: Mercury
gods.description: Roman messenger god of merchants and thieves.

gods.name: Minerva
gods.description: Roman goddess of wisdom and crafts; third of the Capitoline Triad.

gods.name: Apollo (Roman)
gods.description: Imported from the Greek largely without translation — a rare case. Plague-averter consulted during the Second Punic War. Augustus promoted him as imperial patron after his victory at Actium.

gods.name: Diana
gods.description: Roman goddess of the hunt, moon, and wild places.

gods.name: Bacchus
gods.description: Roman Dionysus; central to the Bacchanalia (suppressed by the Senate in 186 BCE).

gods.name: Janus
gods.description: Two-faced god of beginnings and endings. Purely Roman — no Greek equivalent. January is named for him.

gods.name: Quirinus
gods.description: Sabine-origin state god; later identified with deified Romulus. Third of the Archaic Triad (Jupiter, Mars, Quirinus).

gods.name: Faunus
gods.description: Horned forest-god of Latium; ancestor of Latin royalty.

gods.name: Lares
gods.description: Guardian spirits of the household and crossroads. Central to daily Roman piety.

gods.name: Penates
gods.description: Household gods of the pantry and storeroom. Also state-level Penates protected Rome itself.

gods.name: Nun
gods.description: Primordial waters of chaos from which creation emerged. Part of the Ogdoad of Hermopolis.

gods.name: Atum
gods.description: Self-created completion-god who emerged from Nun; spawned Shu and Tefnut by masturbation or spittle. Heliopolitan creator; later merged with Ra as Atum-Ra.

gods.name: Ra
gods.description: Sun-god, chief deity for most of Egyptian history. Travels the sky by day and the underworld by night, battling Apep.

gods.name: Shu
gods.description: God of air; holds the sky (Nut) above the earth (Geb).

gods.name: Tefnut
gods.description: Goddess of moisture; twin-consort of Shu.

gods.name: Geb
gods.description: Earth-god. Unusual for a male earth-god; his laughter causes earthquakes.

gods.name: Nut
gods.description: Sky-goddess arched over Geb, swallowing the sun each evening and giving birth to it at dawn.

gods.name: Osiris
gods.description: Murdered by Set, reassembled by Isis, became judge of the dead. Archetypal dying-and-rising god.

gods.name: Isis
gods.description: Great magician; reassembled Osiris's scattered body. Her cult spread across the Roman Empire.

gods.name: Set
gods.description: Red-haired god of the desert and disorder. Murdered Osiris, battled Horus, but also nightly defends Ra against Apep.
epithets: He Before Whom the Sky Shakes

gods.name: Nephthys
gods.description: Sister-consort of Set. Aided Isis in mourning and protecting Osiris. Mother of Anubis (often by Osiris).

gods.name: Horus
gods.description: Falcon-headed god of kingship; avenged his father Osiris by defeating Set. Each pharaoh was considered Horus incarnate.

gods.name: Hathor
gods.description: Cow-horned sky-goddess of love, music, and motherhood. Sometimes the destructive Eye of Ra (as Sekhmet).

gods.name: Thoth
gods.description: Ibis-headed god of writing, wisdom, and the moon. Scribe of the gods and arbiter of divine disputes.

gods.name: Anubis
gods.description: Jackal-headed god of mummification. Weighed the heart against Maat's feather at judgment.
epithets: He Who is Upon his Mountain

gods.name: Ma'at
gods.description: Personification of truth and cosmic order. Her feather was weighed against every soul's heart.

gods.name: Ptah
gods.description: Memphite creator-god who made the world by thinking and speaking. Patron of craftsmen.

gods.name: Sekhmet
gods.description: Lion-headed goddess of warfare and plague. When unleashed, nearly destroyed humanity until tricked with red beer.

gods.name: Bastet
gods.description: Cat-headed goddess of the home. Fierce protector, softer counterpart to Sekhmet.

gods.name: Khonsu
gods.description: Moon-god of the Theban triad with Amun and Mut.

gods.name: Amun
gods.description: 'The hidden one'. Rose from obscure wind-god to chief deity as Amun-Ra during the New Kingdom.

gods.name: Mut
gods.description: 'Mother'; Theban goddess paired with Amun.

gods.name: Apep (Apophis)
gods.description: Serpent of chaos. Attacked Ra's sun-barque every night; eternally defeated but never destroyed.

gods.name: Imhotep
gods.description: Historical 27th-century BCE architect of the Step Pyramid, deified ~2000 years later as a god of medicine. A true deified human.

gods.name: Apsu
gods.description: Primordial fresh water. Killed by his descendant Ea before he could destroy the younger gods.

gods.name: Tiamat
gods.description: Primordial salt-water. Rose against the younger gods to avenge Apsu; slain by Marduk, her body split to form heaven and earth.

gods.name: Anu
gods.description: Sumerian An, Akkadian Anu — sky-god and supreme authority of the Mesopotamian pantheon. Remote and rarely active.

gods.name: Ki
gods.description: Sumerian earth, consort of An. Mother of Enlil.

gods.name: Enlil
gods.description: God of wind and kingship; chief executive of the pantheon. Decreed the Flood.

gods.name: Enki
gods.description: Akkadian Ea. Clever trickster-wise god of fresh water and mischief. Warned Utnapishtim of the Flood.

gods.name: Ninhursag
gods.description: Great mother-goddess; shaped humans from clay with Enki.

gods.name: Inanna / Ishtar
gods.description: Paradox goddess of love AND war; Queen of Heaven. Associated with the planet Venus (morning and evening stars). Descended to her sister Ereshkigal's underworld; her return required Dumuzi as substitute. Among the oldest recorded deities.

gods.name: Dumuzi / Tammuz
gods.description: Shepherd-god and mortal consort of Inanna. Sent to the underworld half each year — the archetypal dying-and-rising god.

gods.name: Utu / Shamash
gods.description: Sun-god and judge. Gave the law code to Hammurabi. Brother of Inanna.

gods.name: Nanna / Sin
gods.description: Moon-god of Ur. Father of Sun, Venus, and the Queen of the Dead.

gods.name: Ereshkigal
gods.description: Queen of the Mesopotamian underworld (Irkalla). Sister of Inanna, whom she killed on her descent.

gods.name: Nergal
gods.description: God of plague and scorching noonday sun. Descended to the underworld, married Ereshkigal, became co-ruler.

gods.name: Ninurta
gods.description: Warrior-farmer god who slew Anzu and recovered the Tablet of Destinies.

gods.name: Marduk
gods.description: Babylonian storm-king; killed Tiamat and reshaped the cosmos. Rose to head the pantheon as Babylon rose to empire.

gods.name: Nabu
gods.description: God of writing and scribes; kept the Tablet of Destinies.

gods.name: Ashur
gods.description: Chief god of Assyria. As Assyria conquered Babylon, Ashur replaced Marduk in the state-pantheon.

gods.name: Lilitu
gods.description: Night-demoness of Mesopotamian lore. Ancestress of the later Jewish Lilith.

gods.name: Humbaba
gods.description: Giant guardian of the Cedar Forest, slain by Gilgamesh and Enkidu.

gods.name: Gilgamesh
gods.description: Two-thirds divine king of Uruk; historical basis for the hero of the Epic of Gilgamesh, the oldest surviving great work of literature. Probable reign c. 2900–2700 BCE. Deified after death as a judge of the underworld.

gods.name: El
gods.description: Supreme Canaanite patriarch-god. Later identified in early Israelite religion with YHWH ('El Shaddai', 'El Elyon').

gods.name: Athirat / Asherah
gods.description: Consort of El, 'mother of the gods'. Worshipped in early Israel as YHWH's consort, later suppressed by reformers.

gods.name: Baal Hadad
gods.description: Storm-god who defeated Yam (sea) and Mot (death) to claim kingship. Chief rival deity in the Hebrew Bible.

gods.name: Anat
gods.description: Warrior-goddess; avenger of her brother-consort Baal. Waded in blood up to her knees.

gods.name: Astarte
gods.description: Phoenician counterpart of Ishtar. Her cult reached Egypt, Greece (as Aphrodite), and Carthage (as Tanit).

gods.name: Yam
gods.description: Sea-god defeated by Baal in a chaoskampf battle.

gods.name: Mot
gods.description: God of death. Swallowed Baal, who returned after Anat destroyed Mot.

gods.name: Melqart
gods.description: Chief god of Tyre. Greeks identified him with Heracles; his cult spread throughout the Mediterranean.

gods.name: Dagon
gods.description: Grain-god of Philistine and Ugaritic worship. Sometimes listed as Baal's father.

gods.name: Moloch
gods.description: Deity (or ritual title) associated with child sacrifice in biblical polemic. Historical worship debated among scholars.

gods.name: Tarhunt
gods.description: Hittite storm-king. Slew the serpent Illuyanka. Indo-European in origin.

gods.name: Teshub
gods.description: Hurrian storm-god; merged with Tarhunt under Hittite rule.

gods.name: Kumarbi
gods.description: Hurrian father-god who castrated his predecessor Anu — clearly influenced Hesiod's Cronus/Uranus story.

gods.name: Hebat
gods.description: Hurrian queen of heaven, consort of Teshub. Later merged with the Hittite Sun-goddess of Arinna.

gods.name: Telipinu
gods.description: Vanishing god whose disappearance caused drought; the myth of his search is central Hittite ritual.

gods.name: Illuyanka
gods.description: Serpent defeated by Tarhunt — the chaoskampf template of the ancient Near East.

gods.name: Ymir
gods.description: First being, formed from the rime of Ginnungagap. Slain by Odin and brothers; the world was built from his body.

gods.name: Búri
gods.description: First of the Aesir, licked from ice by the cow Audhumla.

gods.name: Bor
gods.description: Son of Búri, father of Odin, Vili, and Ve by the giantess Bestla.

gods.name: Odin
gods.description: All-father of the Aesir. Sacrificed an eye for wisdom, hanged himself on Yggdrasil for the runes. God of kings, poets, madmen, and the dead.

gods.name: Frigg
gods.description: Queen of the Aesir, wife of Odin. Knew all fates but spoke of none. Tried to save Baldr by extracting oaths from all things — missing only mistletoe.

gods.name: Thor
gods.description: Red-bearded thunder-god. Wielded the hammer Mjölnir; defender of Asgard against giants. Fated to kill and be killed by Jörmungandr at Ragnarök.

gods.name: Baldr
gods.description: Most beautiful of the Aesir. Killed by his blind brother Hodr (manipulated by Loki) via mistletoe. Returns after Ragnarök.

gods.name: Týr
gods.description: God of justice and oaths. Gave his right hand to bind the wolf Fenrir. Likely originally the PIE sky-father (*Tiwaz < *Dyēus).

gods.name: Loki
gods.description: Jötunn-born trickster counted among the Aesir. Mother (by Svadilfari) of Odin's steed Sleipnir. Instigates Ragnarök.

gods.name: Heimdall
gods.description: Watchman of the Bifröst; will sound Gjallarhorn at Ragnarök. Also, as Rig, originator of human social classes.

gods.name: Bragi
gods.description: God of poetry, husband of Idunn.

gods.name: Idunn
gods.description: Keeper of the golden apples that keep the gods young.

gods.name: Njörd
gods.description: Vanir god of the sea and wealth. Exchanged to the Aesir as hostage after the Aesir-Vanir war.

gods.name: Freyr
gods.description: Vanir god of fertility, peace, and harvest. Gave up his sword for love; fated to fall to Surt at Ragnarök.

gods.name: Freyja
gods.description: Vanir goddess of love, fertility, and seidr-magic. Claims half the battle-dead (Odin gets the other half).

gods.name: Skadi
gods.description: Jötunn of winter who married into the Aesir to avenge her father.

gods.name: Hel
gods.description: Half-living, half-corpse daughter of Loki. Rules the underworld realm sharing her name.

gods.name: Fenrir
gods.description: Monstrous wolf child of Loki. Bound by the gods; fated to kill Odin at Ragnarök.

gods.name: Jörmungandr
gods.description: Midgard-encircling serpent. Fated to kill Thor at Ragnarök, and be killed by him.

gods.name: Sleipnir
gods.description: Odin's eight-legged horse, birthed by Loki in mare form.

gods.name: Brahma
gods.description: Creator-god of the Trimurti; associated with creation, the Vedas, and identified with the earlier Vedic Prajapati. Born from Vishnu's navel-lotus. Rarely worshipped directly — his one major temple is at Pushkar in Rajasthan.

gods.name: Vishnu
gods.description: Preserver of the Trimurti. Descends in avatars (Rama, Krishna, Buddha, Kalki, and others) to restore dharma.

gods.name: Shiva
gods.description: Destroyer-transformer of the Trimurti. Meditates on Kailash; dances the Tandava. Paradoxically ascetic and erotic.

gods.name: Saraswati
gods.description: Goddess of learning, music, and speech. Consort of Brahma.

gods.name: Lakshmi
gods.description: Goddess of wealth and fortune. Consort of Vishnu, born from the churning of the ocean.

gods.name: Parvati
gods.description: Mountain goddess; consort of Shiva. Mother of Ganesha and Kartikeya. Manifests as fierce Durga and Kali.

gods.name: Durga
gods.description: Principal aspect of Mahadevi in the Shakta tradition — regarded there as the Supreme Goddess herself. Fierce warrior-form of Parvati; slew the buffalo-demon Mahishasura over nine nights, the origin of Navaratri.

gods.name: Kali
gods.description: Goddess of time, death, and destruction. Wears a garland of skulls and a skirt of severed arms. First of the ten Mahavidyas; central to Tantric and Kalikula traditions. Ultimately liberating — destroyer of ego and illusion.

gods.name: Ganesha
gods.description: Elephant-headed remover of obstacles and god of beginnings. Beheaded by Shiva and given an elephant's head. Invoked before any undertaking — the first to be worshipped in any rite (Pratham Pujya).

gods.name: Kartikeya (Skanda)
gods.description: Six-faced commander of the divine army. Major deity in Tamil Hinduism as Murugan.

gods.name: Indra
gods.description: King of the Devas in the Vedic period. Slew the dragon Vritra with his vajra. Later eclipsed by the Trimurti.

gods.name: Agni
gods.description: Fire god; carrier of offerings between worlds. One of the most-hymned deities in the Rigveda.

gods.name: Surya
gods.description: Sun-god. Father of Yama (death), Manu (first man), and Karna (hero of the Mahabharata).

gods.name: Chandra (Soma)
gods.description: Moon-god, married to 27 lunar mansions. Overlaps with Soma, the ritual intoxicant.

gods.name: Yama
gods.description: First mortal; became first lord of the dead and judge of the departed. Cognate to Iranian Yima.

gods.name: Varuna
gods.description: Vedic guardian of rta (cosmic order), demoted to sea-god in later Hinduism. Cognate to Iranian Ahura Mazda.

gods.name: Kamadeva
gods.description: Hindu god of love. Shoots flower-arrows that kindle desire.

gods.name: Hanuman
gods.description: Monkey-god of the Ramayana — vanara ally and devoted companion of Rama. Son of the wind-god Vayu; considered an incarnation of Shiva in some traditions. Embodiment of selfless devotion (bhakti).

gods.name: Rama
gods.description: Seventh and most popular avatar of Vishnu; hero-king of the Ramayana. Prince of Ayodhya, exiled for fourteen years; his wife Sita was abducted by the ten-headed Ravana, sparking the war central to the epic. Upheld as the model of righteous kingship (maryāda puruṣottama).

gods.name: Krishna
gods.description: Eighth avatar of Vishnu. Charioteer-teacher of the Bhagavad Gita; playful cowherd of Vrindavan.

gods.name: Vritra
gods.description: Drought-dragon slain by Indra — the paradigmatic Indo-European serpent-slaying myth.

gods.name: Dyaus Pitar
gods.description: Vedic sky-father, cognate with Zeus/Jupiter. Already fading by the Rigveda.

gods.name: Prithvi
gods.description: Vedic earth-mother, paired with Dyaus. Later absorbed as Bhudevi, consort of Vishnu.

gods.name: Ushas
gods.description: Vedic dawn-goddess; cognate with Eos and Aurora.

gods.name: Ahura Mazda
gods.description: The 'Wise Lord' — supreme creator god of Zoroastrianism. Uncreated. Eternally opposed to Angra Mainyu.

gods.name: Angra Mainyu
gods.description: Destructive spirit, eternal adversary of Ahura Mazda. Created the demons (daevas). Will be defeated at the end of time.

gods.name: Mithra
gods.description: God of covenants and light. Shared origin with Vedic Mitra. Spawned the Roman cult of Mithras.

gods.name: Anahita
gods.description: Goddess of waters, fertility, and wisdom. Major cult figure under the Achaemenids and Sassanians.

gods.name: Sraosha
gods.description: Divine 'listening' or conscience; guide of souls across the Chinvat Bridge.

gods.name: Yima
gods.description: First king and first mortal (cognate to Vedic Yama). Built an underground refuge against a coming winter.

gods.name: Zarathustra
gods.description: Historical prophet-founder of Zoroastrianism (dates debated, possibly c. 1500–1000 BCE).

gods.name: The Dagda
gods.description: 'Good God' — father-figure of the Tuatha Dé Danann. Owned a cauldron of plenty, a magical club, and a harp that ordered the seasons.

gods.name: Lugh
gods.description: 'Master of all skills'. Led the Tuatha Dé Danann to victory over the Fomorians. Father of the hero Cú Chulainn.

gods.name: The Morrígan
gods.description: Crow-goddess of battle and sovereignty. Triple goddess with Badb and Macha (or Nemain).

gods.name: Brigid
gods.description: Goddess of poetry, smithcraft, and healing. Her cult continued into Christian Ireland as Saint Brigid.

gods.name: Danu
gods.description: Ancestor-goddess of the Tuatha Dé Danann ('people of Danu'). May be cognate with Welsh Dôn.

gods.name: Nuada
gods.description: First king of the Tuatha Dé Danann. Lost his arm in battle — Celtic kings could not have bodily defect — and was temporarily replaced by Bres.

gods.name: Ogma
gods.description: Inventor of the Ogham script. Champion warrior and orator.

gods.name: Manannán mac Lir
gods.description: Sea-god and ruler of the Otherworld. Drove a chariot across waves. Welsh counterpart: Manawydan.

gods.name: Aengus Óg
gods.description: Young god of love. His swan-form lover Caer Ibormeith was won after a long search.

gods.name: Cú Chulainn
gods.description: Hero of the Ulster Cycle. Unleashed the battle-frenzy 'warp spasm'. Died young, tied to a standing stone.

gods.name: Dôn
gods.description: Welsh mother-goddess; ancestor of many Welsh divine figures. Cognate with Irish Danu.

gods.name: Gwydion
gods.description: Magician-trickster of the Fourth Branch of the Mabinogi. Created Blodeuwedd from flowers.

gods.name: Arianrhod
gods.description: 'Silver wheel' — cosmic goddess of stars and fate. Cursed her son Lleu three times.

gods.name: Lleu Llaw Gyffes
gods.description: Welsh Lugh. Killed by Blodeuwedd and Gronw Pebr, resurrected by Gwydion.

gods.name: Rhiannon
gods.description: Horse-goddess of the First Branch of the Mabinogi. Rode a horse no rider could catch; unjustly accused of killing her son.

gods.name: Pryderi
gods.description: Prince who appears in all four branches of the Mabinogi.

gods.name: Brân the Blessed
gods.description: Giant-king of Britain in the Second Branch. His head, buried under the Tower of London, protects Britain.

gods.name: Branwen
gods.description: Queen whose abuse by her Irish husband triggered a war that devastated Ireland.

gods.name: Perun
gods.description: Chief thunder-god of the Slavs. Topped Vladimir's pagan pantheon in Kyiv. Opposed by the serpent Veles.

gods.name: Veles
gods.description: Chthonic 'horned' god of cattle, magic, and the underworld. Eternal adversary of Perun — the Slavic chaoskampf.

gods.name: Svarog
gods.description: Smith-sky-god, creator or ancestor. Father of Dazhbog.

gods.name: Dazhbog
gods.description: 'Giving god' — sun-god and source of wealth.

gods.name: Mokosh
gods.description: Moist-earth-mother. Only female deity in Vladimir's pantheon. Her worship persisted under the Christian cult of Mary.

gods.name: Yarilo
gods.description: Spring-god who dies each winter and returns each spring. Consort-brother of Morana.

gods.name: Morana
gods.description: Goddess of winter and death. Ritually 'drowned' at the end of winter.

gods.name: Chernobog
gods.description: 'Black god' mentioned by medieval Christian chroniclers. Paired (disputed) with Belobog.

gods.name: Ukko
gods.description: Chief Finnish sky-thunder god. 'Ukkonen' (thunder) in modern Finnish still bears his name.

gods.name: Akka
gods.description: 'Old woman' — earth-mother, consort of Ukko.

gods.name: Väinämöinen
gods.description: Eternal sage-bard, central hero of the Kalevala. Creates the world with his songs.

gods.name: Ilmarinen
gods.description: Eternal smith; forged the sky-dome and the mythic Sampo.

gods.name: Louhi
gods.description: Witch-queen of the northern land Pohjola; Kalevala antagonist.

gods.name: Lemminkäinen
gods.description: Reckless hero of the Kalevala. Killed at Tuonela (the underworld) and resurrected by his mother.

gods.name: Tapio
gods.description: Forest-king; hunters prayed to him.

gods.name: Tinia
gods.description: Chief Etruscan sky-god; equivalent to Zeus/Jupiter.

gods.name: Uni
gods.description: Etruscan queen of the gods, absorbed into the Roman Juno.

gods.name: Menrva
gods.description: Etruscan goddess of wisdom and strategic war — source of Roman Minerva.

gods.name: Aita
gods.description: Etruscan lord of the underworld.

gods.name: Voltumna
gods.description: Chief god of the Etruscan League; source of Roman Vertumnus.

gods.name: Amaterasu
gods.description: Sun-goddess and chief kami of the Shinto pantheon; ancestor of the Imperial Family via Ninigi. Withdrew into the Heavenly Rock Cave after Susanoo's rampage, plunging the world into darkness until coaxed out by Ame-no-Uzume's dance.

gods.name: Tsukuyomi
gods.description: Moon-god; estranged from Amaterasu after killing a food-goddess, explaining the separation of day and night.

gods.name: Susanoo
gods.description: Storm-god. Exiled for rampaging; slew the eight-headed serpent Yamata-no-Orochi; recovered the sword Kusanagi.

gods.name: Izanagi
gods.description: Creator-god. With Izanami, churned the primal sea to form the Japanese islands.

gods.name: Izanami
gods.description: Creator-goddess, later queen of Yomi (underworld) after dying giving birth to Kagutsuchi.

gods.name: Kagutsuchi
gods.description: Fire-god whose birth killed Izanami. Beheaded by Izanagi; new gods emerged from his spilled blood.

gods.name: Inari Ōkami
gods.description: Gender-variable kami of rice and prosperity. Messenger foxes (kitsune) serve Inari. One-third of Japan's shrines are Inari shrines.

gods.name: Raijin
gods.description: Thunder-god who beats drums to produce thunder.

gods.name: Fūjin
gods.description: Wind-god depicted with a great bag from which he releases storms.

gods.name: Ōkuninushi
gods.description: Builder of the land; ceded rule to Amaterasu's descendants. Still honored at Izumo Shrine.

gods.name: Hachiman
gods.description: War-god, patron of the samurai. Often identified with deified Emperor Ōjin.

gods.name: Yamata no Orochi
gods.description: Eight-headed, eight-tailed serpent slain by Susanoo; the sword Kusanagi was found in its tail.

gods.name: Hwanin
gods.description: 'Lord of Heaven' in Korean foundation myth; father of Hwanung. Identified with the Buddhist Śakra and the Jade Emperor.

gods.name: Hwanung
gods.description: Son of Hwanin; descended to Mount Taebaek with 3,000 followers to teach humanity law and agriculture.

gods.name: Ungnyeo
gods.description: Bear transformed into a woman after enduring 21 days with garlic and mugwort in a cave. Mother of Dangun.

gods.name: Dangun Wanggeom
gods.description: Founder of Gojoseon (traditionally 2333 BCE), the first Korean kingdom. Reigned 1,500 years, then became a mountain spirit.

gods.name: Samshin Halmoni
gods.description: Three grandmother-goddesses of birth and child-rearing. Central to Korean folk religion.

gods.name: Yongwang
gods.description: Dragon Kings of the four seas — Korean counterparts of the Chinese Longwang.

gods.name: Sanshin
gods.description: Mountain-spirits, one per mountain. Often depicted as an old man with a tiger.

gods.name: Jeoseung Saja
gods.description: Black-robed messengers from the underworld who escort souls to the afterlife.

gods.name: Yu Huang (Jade Emperor)
gods.description: Jade Emperor — supreme ruler of heaven in Chinese folk religion. Head of the celestial bureaucracy.

gods.name: Xiwangmu
gods.description: Queen Mother of the West; tends the peaches of immortality.

gods.name: Pangu
gods.description: Primordial giant who separated heaven from earth; the world was made from his body.

gods.name: Nüwa
gods.description: Serpent-bodied goddess who created humans from clay and repaired the collapsed sky with colored stones.

gods.name: Fuxi
gods.description: Serpent-bodied culture-hero; gave humans the trigrams of the I Ching. First of the mythical Three Sovereigns.

gods.name: Shangdi
gods.description: 'Supreme Deity' — chief god of Shang and early Zhou. Later identified with impersonal Heaven (Tian).

gods.name: Xihe
gods.description: Mother of ten suns, one for each day of the ancient ten-day week.

gods.name: Hou Yi
gods.description: Archer who shot down nine of the ten suns to save the earth from scorching.

gods.name: Chang'e
gods.description: Moon-goddess. Consumed the elixir of immortality and fled to the moon. Mid-Autumn Festival honors her.

gods.name: Dragon Kings
gods.description: Four Dragon Kings rule the four seas. Sources of rain, prayed to in droughts.

gods.name: Guanyin
gods.description: Goddess of mercy. Chinese form of the bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara; underwent gender-shift on entering East Asia.

gods.name: Zao Jun
gods.description: Kitchen god. Reports each household's conduct to the Jade Emperor at year's end.

gods.name: Olodumare
gods.description: Supreme creator, beyond gender and rarely approached directly. Delegated creation and governance to the orishas.

gods.name: Obatala
gods.description: Sky-father orisha delegated to shape humans from clay; sometimes drunk while working, explaining birth defects.

gods.name: Yemaya
gods.description: Mother of all orishas; goddess of the Ogun river (and in the diaspora, the ocean).

gods.name: Shango
gods.description: Thunder-god and legendary fourth Alaafin of Oyo. Historical king who became an orisha.

gods.name: Oshun
gods.description: River-goddess of love and beauty. Central to Cuban Santería.

gods.name: Ogun
gods.description: Orisha of iron, warfare, and technology. First among the orishas to descend to earth, cutting a path with his machete.

gods.name: Oya
gods.description: Storm-wind orisha; gatekeeper between life and death.

gods.name: Eshu
gods.description: Messenger between humans and orishas. Trickster at crossroads. Offerings to Eshu precede those to any other orisha.

gods.name: Oxossi
gods.description: Hunter-orisha, associated with forests and knowledge found there.

gods.name: Babalú-Ayé
gods.description: Orisha of smallpox and skin disease; also of healing from them.

gods.name: Ometeotl
gods.description: Dual-gendered primordial deity, Ometecuhtli-Omecihuatl. Source of all existence.

gods.name: Tezcatlipoca
gods.description: 'Smoking Mirror' — associated with the night sky, hurricanes, obsidian, and conflict. One of four sons of Ometeotl. Ruled earlier world-ages; eternal rival and counterweight to Quetzalcoatl. His obsidian mirror let him see anywhere.

gods.name: Quetzalcoatl
gods.description: Feathered Serpent. Created humans by bringing bones from the underworld. Self-immolated to become Venus.

gods.name: Huitzilopochtli
gods.description: War and sun-god; patron of the Mexica. Born fully-armed to defend Coatlicue from Coyolxauhqui.

gods.name: Xipe Totec
gods.description: 'Flayed Lord' — agricultural god of vegetation-renewal. Priests wore flayed skins in his festival.

gods.name: Coatlicue
gods.description: 'Serpent-skirt' — earth goddess who miraculously conceived Huitzilopochtli by a ball of feathers. Her daughter attempted matricide in response.

gods.name: Coyolxauhqui
gods.description: Moon-goddess, killed and dismembered by her infant brother Huitzilopochtli.

gods.name: Tlaloc
gods.description: Rain-god. Children sacrificed to him — their tears symbolized rainfall.

gods.name: Chalchiuhtlicue
gods.description: 'Jade skirt' — goddess of lakes and streams. Ruled the world-age destroyed by flood.

gods.name: Mictlantecuhtli
gods.description: Skeletal lord of Mictlan, the Aztec underworld.

gods.name: Xochiquetzal
gods.description: 'Flower-quetzal' — young goddess of love, art, and flowers.

gods.name: Mixcoatl
gods.description: Cloud-serpent; god of the hunt and the Milky Way.

gods.name: Tonatiuh
gods.description: Sun of the current (fifth) world-age. Required blood to continue rising.

gods.name: Huehuecóyotl
gods.description: 'Old coyote' — trickster god of music and mischief.

gods.name: Itzamná
gods.description: Supreme creator-god; source of writing and the calendar.

gods.name: Ix Chel
gods.description: Moon-goddess and patron of weaving, healing, and childbirth.

gods.name: Kukulkan
gods.description: Feathered Serpent; Maya equivalent of Quetzalcoatl. The shadow-serpent appears on El Castillo at equinoxes.

gods.name: Chaac
gods.description: Rain-god. Four Chaacs correspond to the four cardinal directions.

gods.name: Hunahpu
gods.description: One of the Hero Twins of the Popol Vuh. Defeated the lords of Xibalba and became the sun.

gods.name: Xbalanque
gods.description: Twin of Hunahpu; became the moon.

gods.name: Camazotz
gods.description: Death-bat god of Xibalba. Beheaded Hunahpu before his resurrection.

gods.name: Kinich Ahau
gods.description: Sun-god; often considered an aspect of Itzamná.

gods.name: Inti
gods.description: Sun-god and divine ancestor of the Sapa Inca.

gods.name: Viracocha
gods.description: Creator-god; taught humans civilization and then disappeared across the Pacific.

gods.name: Pachamama
gods.description: Earth-mother of the Andes, still honored today with offerings of food and alcohol.

gods.name: Mama Killa
gods.description: Silver moon-mother. Eclipses were feared as her being devoured.

gods.name: Supay
gods.description: Lord of Uku Pacha (underworld) and later patron of miners.

gods.name: Ranginui
gods.description: Sky-father of Maori cosmology. Separated from Papa by their children, longing still causes rain (his tears).

gods.name: Papatūānuku
gods.description: Earth-mother; mists rising from her are her sighs for Ranginui.

gods.name: Tāne
gods.description: God of forests. Pushed his parents apart; fashioned the first woman from red earth.

gods.name: Tangaroa
gods.description: Sea-god. Widely worshipped across Polynesia.

gods.name: Tūmatauenga
gods.description: War-god; ancestor of humanity. Fought his siblings, establishing human dominion over nature.

gods.name: Māui
gods.description: Demigod trickster; fished up the Polynesian islands, slowed the sun, and died trying to conquer death.

gods.name: Pele
gods.description: Hawaiian volcano-goddess. Lives in Kīlauea. Creates Hawaiian islands through eruptions.

gods.name: Hine-nui-te-pō
gods.description: Great goddess of death. Crushed Maui when he tried to steal her heart and win immortality.

gods.name: YHWH
gods.description: The God of Israel. Unpronounceable tetragrammaton. Earlier Israelite religion showed influence from Canaanite El; later strictly monotheistic.

gods.name: Michael
gods.description: Chief archangel; warrior against evil. Appears in Daniel, Jude, and Revelation.

gods.name: Gabriel
gods.description: Messenger archangel. Revealed prophecy to Daniel, announced Jesus's birth to Mary, recited Qur'an to Muhammad.

gods.name: Raphael
gods.description: Healing archangel; major figure in the Book of Tobit.

gods.name: Uriel
gods.description: Archangel of light and wisdom. Prominent in 2 Esdras and Enochic literature.

gods.name: Metatron
gods.description: Highest angel in later Jewish mysticism. In some traditions, the ascended Enoch transformed into an angel.

gods.name: Satan
gods.description: In the Hebrew Bible, 'the accuser' in the heavenly court. Evolved into Christianity's adversary of God.

gods.name: Lilith
gods.description: Night-demon of rabbinic lore. Alphabet of Ben Sira made her Adam's first wife, rejected for demanding equality.

gods.name: Asmodeus
gods.description: Demon prominent in Tobit. Ruler of demons in later Christian demonology.

gods.name: Trinity
gods.description: One God in three persons: Father, Son (Jesus Christ), and Holy Spirit. Defined at Nicaea (325) and Constantinople (381).

gods.name: Jesus Christ
gods.description: In Christian theology, the incarnate second person of the Trinity. Historical Jesus (c. 4 BCE – 30 CE) crucified under Pilate.

gods.name: Mary
gods.description: Mother of Jesus. Revered especially in Catholic and Orthodox traditions as Theotokos ('God-bearer').

gods.name: Holy Spirit
gods.description: Third person of the Trinity. Agent of sanctification and inspiration. Descended at Pentecost.

gods.name: Lucifer / Devil
gods.description: In Christian tradition, the fallen highest angel. Identified with Satan and (via mistranslation) Isaiah 14's 'morning star'.

gods.name: Allah
gods.description: 'The God' — supreme and only deity of Islam. Same God as of Judaism and Christianity in Islamic theology. Strictly unitary (tawhid).

gods.name: Jibrīl (Gabriel)
gods.description: Archangel who delivered the Qur'an to Muhammad.

gods.name: Mīkā'īl
gods.description: Archangel of rain, plants, and provision.

gods.name: Isrāfīl
gods.description: Archangel who will blow the trumpet on Judgment Day.

gods.name: Azrael
gods.description: Angel of death, who takes souls at their appointed hour.

gods.name: Iblīs
gods.description: Jinn who refused to bow before Adam and was cursed. Father of the shayatin (devils).

gods.name: Muhammad
gods.description: Historical prophet (c. 570–632 CE). Final prophet in Islam. Recipient of the Qur'an. Not divine, but the 'seal of the prophets'.

gods.name: Augustus
gods.description: 63 BCE – 14 CE. First Roman emperor. Deified after death (Divus Augustus), beginning the imperial cult. Already had temples during his life in the eastern provinces.

gods.name: Julius Caesar
gods.description: 100–44 BCE. First Roman officially deified (Divus Julius, 42 BCE), opening the door to imperial cult.

gods.name: Alexander the Great
gods.description: 356–323 BCE. Declared son of Zeus-Ammon at Siwa. Worshipped during his lifetime in Egypt and after across the Hellenistic world.

gods.name: Imhotep (deification)
gods.description: ~2600 BCE architect of the Step Pyramid. Deified ~2000 years later as a god of medicine and wisdom — one of history's clearest human-to-god elevations.

gods.name: Guan Yu
gods.description: ~160–220 CE. Chinese general of the Three Kingdoms. Deified as Guan Di, god of war and brotherhood. Still widely worshipped, including by police and triads alike.

gods.name: Confucius
gods.description: 551–479 BCE. Historical philosopher. Received divine titles and temple worship from the Han dynasty onward.

gods.name: Laozi
gods.description: Semi-legendary c. 6th-century BCE sage. Deified in Taoism as Taishang Laojun, one of the Three Pure Ones.

gods.name: Mazu
gods.description: Historical Lin Moniang (960–987 CE), a Fujian shaman girl. Deified as sea-goddess; among the most widely-worshipped figures in Chinese folk religion.

gods.name: Tenjin
gods.description: Deified Sugawara no Michizane (845–903 CE), scholar-minister unjustly exiled. After disasters following his death, enshrined as kami of scholarship.

gods.name: Ōjin / Hachiman
gods.description: Semi-legendary 15th emperor of Japan (traditionally r. 270–310). Deified as Hachiman, patron of samurai.

gods.name: Siddhartha Gautama
gods.description: c. 563–483 BCE (debated). Founder of Buddhism. Not a 'god' in original Buddhism, but venerated and iconographically deified in Mahayana and folk practice.

gods.name: Asclepius (historical layer)
gods.description: Possibly a historical 12th-century BCE physician elevated to god of medicine; also listed as Greek deity above.

gods.name: Romulus
gods.description: Legendary founder and first king of Rome. Deified as Quirinus after disappearing in a storm.

gods.name: Antinous
gods.description: c. 111–130 CE. Bithynian youth and companion of Emperor Hadrian. After drowning in the Nile, deified by Hadrian; his cult spread widely.

gods.name: King Sejong the Great
gods.description: 1397–1450. 4th Joseon king, created the Hangul script. Enshrined and venerated rather than fully deified, but illustrates East Asian elevation of sage-rulers.

gods.name: Dievas
gods.description: Baltic sky-father. Directly cognate with Dyēus. The Christian God in Lithuanian/Latvian is still called Dievas/Dievs.

gods.name: Perkūnas
gods.description: Thunder-god. The most direct surviving continuation of *Perkʷunos. Chases the devil Velnias across the sky.

gods.name: Saulė
gods.description: Baltic sun-goddess. In mythology, separated from her husband the moon after he was unfaithful with the morning star.

gods.name: Mēness
gods.description: Moon-god. Split by Perkūnas for abandoning Saulė for the dawn-star Aušrinė — explaining the phases of the moon.

gods.name: Aušrinė
gods.description: Morning-star goddess. Cognate with Vedic Ushas and Greek Eos.

gods.name: Laima
gods.description: Goddess of fate. Visits each newborn to determine their life's course.

gods.name: Žemyna
gods.description: Lithuanian earth-mother. Blessed at every meal with a libation of beer.

gods.name: Velnias
gods.description: Chthonic horned god; constantly chased and struck by Perkūnas. Cognate to Slavic Veles.

gods.name: Giltinė
gods.description: Death-goddess; sister of Laima. Strangles the dying with her long blue tongue.

gods.name: Gabija
gods.description: Lithuanian hearth-fire goddess.

gods.name: Aramazd
gods.description: Chief Armenian god. Armenian form of Ahura Mazda, equated by Greeks with Zeus.

gods.name: Anahit
gods.description: Great Armenian mother-goddess. Derived from Iranian Anahita; equated by Greeks with Artemis.

gods.name: Vahagn
gods.description: Dragon-slaying war-god. Direct continuation of Iranian Verethragna. Born of reed-stalks; equated by Greeks with Heracles.

gods.name: Astłik
gods.description: 'Little star' — Armenian love-goddess and consort of Vahagn. Vardavar festival survives as a Christian water-festival.

gods.name: Mihr
gods.description: Armenian form of Mithra. Equated by Greeks with Hephaestus in late antiquity.

gods.name: Nané
gods.description: Daughter of Aramazd, goddess of war and wisdom. Equated by Greeks with Athena.

gods.name: Tir
gods.description: Scribe-god of Aramazd. Equated by Greeks with Apollo or Hermes.

gods.name: Spandaramet
gods.description: Earth / underworld goddess. In later Christian usage, came to mean 'hell' itself.

gods.name: Kamuy-huci
gods.description: Hearth-goddess; the most important kamuy of the home. All other kamuy reach the household through her fire.

gods.name: Chup-Kamuy
gods.description: 'Luminary kamuy'. Sometimes sun, sometimes moon; some traditions treat them as a pair.

gods.name: Kim-un-Kamuy
gods.description: 'Mountain kamuy' — the bear. The iomante (bear ceremony) sends a captive bear cub back to this kamuy with gifts.

gods.name: Repun-Kamuy
gods.description: Sea kamuy — appears as an orca. Drives whales toward Ainu coasts.

gods.name: Kotan-kar-Kamuy
gods.description: 'World-maker kamuy'. Creator of Ainu Mosir (the human world).

gods.name: Aynurakkur
gods.description: Culture-hero who taught the Ainu hunting, fishing, and ritual.

gods.name: Tonpa Shenrab
gods.description: Traditional founder of Bon, said to have lived thousands of years before the Buddha. Parallels Buddhist hagiography.

gods.name: Sipe Gyalmo
gods.description: Queen of the World — chief protector-goddess of Bon.

gods.name: Kuntu Zangpo
gods.description: 'All-good' — primordial Buddha-figure in Bon (and Nyingma Buddhism). Undifferentiated awareness.

gods.name: Palden Lhamo
gods.description: Wrathful protector-goddess. Rides a mule across a sea of blood; shared with Tibetan Buddhism.

gods.name: Drenpa Namkha
gods.description: Great 8th-century Bon sage who outwardly converted to Buddhism to preserve Bon teachings secretly.

gods.name: Yeshe Walmo
gods.description: Wisdom-protector goddess of the Bon medical tradition.

gods.name: Tengri
gods.description: Eternal Blue Sky. Supreme god of the steppe peoples. Mandate of the khagans came from Tengri.

gods.name: Umay
gods.description: Mother-goddess of fertility and children. Attested in Orkhon inscriptions; still invoked in Turkic and Mongol folklore.

gods.name: Erlik
gods.description: Ruler of the underworld. In some traditions a fallen brother of Ulgen; influenced by Zoroastrian dualism.

gods.name: Ülgen
gods.description: Benevolent creator-god of the upper world in Altai tradition; opposed to Erlik.

gods.name: Kayra
gods.description: Remote primordial creator in some Altai traditions; above both Ülgen and Erlik.

gods.name: Ay
gods.description: Moon — Ay-Tengri. Sometimes paired with Kün (Sun) as siblings or spouses.

gods.name: Kün
gods.description: Sun goddess of the Turkic peoples.

gods.name: Yer-Sub
gods.description: 'Earth-water' — the homeland itself, venerated as a pair-deity or spirit. Essential to Turkic/Mongol ritual life.

gods.name: Ngọc Hoàng
gods.description: Vietnamese Jade Emperor; supreme celestial ruler. Local adaptation of Chinese Yu Huang with distinct folkloric additions.

gods.name: Lạc Long Quân
gods.description: Dragon-lord; legendary father of the Vietnamese people by the mountain-fairy Âu Cơ.

gods.name: Âu Cơ
gods.description: Mountain-fairy; legendary mother of the Vietnamese people. Bore 100 eggs, from which came 100 sons — the ancestors of the Bách Việt.

gods.name: Thánh Mẫu
gods.description: Holy Mother — central figure of the Đạo Mẫu four-mothers tradition. Patron goddess of Vietnamese ethno-religion.

gods.name: Sơn Tinh
gods.description: Mountain-spirit. Eternal rival of Thủy Tinh — their annual battles explain Red River flooding.

gods.name: Thủy Tinh
gods.description: Water-spirit; rival of Sơn Tinh. Each year he summons floods in his failed attempt to claim Princess Mỵ Nương.

gods.name: Hubal
gods.description: Chief pre-Islamic idol in the Kaaba at Mecca. Consulted by arrow-divination. Destroyed when Muhammad captured Mecca.

gods.name: Al-Lāt
gods.description: Great Arabian mother-goddess. Named in the Qur'an as one of three goddesses renounced (53:19–20).

gods.name: Al-ʿUzzā
gods.description: 'The mighty' — planet Venus. One of the three pre-Islamic high-goddesses.

gods.name: Manāt
gods.description: Goddess of fate and death. Oldest of the three pre-Islamic goddesses.

gods.name: Dushara
gods.description: Chief god of the Nabataeans at Petra; patron of the royal house.

gods.name: Wadd
gods.description: South Arabian moon-god associated with love. Mentioned in the Qur'an (71:23) as a Noah-era idol.

gods.name: ʿAthtar
gods.description: South Arabian morning-star god; masculine counterpart of Ishtar/Astarte.

gods.name: Allāh (pre-Islamic)
gods.description: 'The God' — already known in pre-Islamic Arabia as the high creator-god, but not the sole object of worship. Islam made him the only god.

gods.name: Wakan Tanka
gods.description: 'Great Mystery' — the sacred totality encompassing all spirits and powers. Not a personal deity in the Abrahamic sense.

gods.name: Pté Ska Wiŋ
gods.description: White Buffalo Calf Woman. Brought the sacred pipe and the seven sacred rites to the Lakota.

gods.name: Inyan
gods.description: Rock — primordial spirit. Opened his veins to create the earth and sky.

gods.name: Maka
gods.description: Earth — created from Inyan's blood.

gods.name: Skan
gods.description: Sky-power; the source of life and motion.

gods.name: Wi
gods.description: Sun. First chief of the gods.

gods.name: Haŋwí
gods.description: Moon — wife of Wi.

gods.name: Iktomi
gods.description: Spider-trickster. Named things to give them being; both culture-bringer and danger.

gods.name: Asdzą́ą́ Nádleehé
gods.description: Changing Woman. Born of Earth and Sky; ages and renews with the seasons. Mother of the Hero Twins.

gods.name: Jóhonaaʼéí
gods.description: Sun-carrier. Father of the Hero Twins by Changing Woman.

gods.name: Naayééʼ Neizghání
gods.description: Elder Hero Twin. Slew the Anaye (alien monsters) that threatened the Fifth World.

gods.name: Tóbájíshchíní
gods.description: Younger Hero Twin. Companion of Monster-Slayer.

gods.name: Na'ashjé'ii Asdzáá
gods.description: Spider Grandmother. Guided the Hero Twins to their father; taught humans weaving.

gods.name: Áłtsé Hastiin
gods.description: First Man — emerged with First Woman into the successive worlds.

gods.name: Áłtsé Asdzáá
gods.description: First Woman. Found the abandoned turquoise figure that became Changing Woman.

gods.name: Mąʼii
gods.description: Coyote — disruptive trickster whose missteps brought death, mortality, and variety into the world.

gods.name: Awenhai
gods.description: Fell from the sky-world, carried on the back of Great Turtle. The earth grew on Turtle's back from mud gathered by animals.

gods.name: Iouskeha
gods.description: The Good Twin / Good Mind. Created useful plants, rivers flowing in both directions (before Flint interfered).

gods.name: Tawiscara
gods.description: The Bad Twin / Evil Mind. Broke his way out of his mother's side, killing her. Created monsters, reversed rivers to flow one way.

gods.name: Dioheʼkó
gods.description: The Three Sisters — Corn, Beans, and Squash. Interplanted in Iroquois gardens, they protect each other and together provide complete nutrition.

gods.name: Grandmother Moon
gods.description: Honored in monthly ceremonies. Sister of the sun.

gods.name: Mawu
gods.description: Moon-goddess, creator-mother. With twin Lisa, children of Nana Buluku.

gods.name: Lisa
gods.description: Sun-god, twin-consort of Mawu. Together Mawu-Lisa often regarded as a single dual-gendered deity.

gods.name: Nana Buluku
gods.description: Primordial grandmother; created the world then handed it to Mawu-Lisa. Remote and rarely approached directly.

gods.name: Dan
gods.description: Rainbow-serpent circling the world. Holds the cosmos together. Survives in Haitian Vodou as Damballa.

gods.name: Ayida-Weddo
gods.description: Rainbow-serpent consort of Dan. Became Ayida-Wedo in Haitian Vodou.

gods.name: Xevioso
gods.description: Thunder-god. Punishes evil with lightning. Known as Sobo / Xevioso in Haiti.

gods.name: Sakpata
gods.description: Earth-orisha-equivalent of disease and healing. Paralleled by Yoruba Babalú-Ayé.

gods.name: Gu
gods.description: Iron-god. Fon counterpart of Yoruba Ogun.

gods.name: Legba
gods.description: Youngest child of Mawu-Lisa; translator between humans and vodun. Stood at every crossroads; first to receive offerings.

gods.name: uNkulunkulu
gods.description: 'Old-old one' — first human who emerged from the reed-bed. Source of humanity and teacher of custom; now a remote creator.

gods.name: uMvelinqangi
gods.description: 'First to appear' — primordial sky-father in some accounts; father or precursor of Unkulunkulu.

gods.name: Nomkhubulwane
gods.description: Princess of heaven. Rain-goddess invoked for agricultural fertility.

gods.name: uThixo
gods.description: Remote sky-god; in modern usage borrowed as the word for the Christian God.

gods.name: Ñamandú
gods.description: Primordial father. Created himself from darkness, then spoke the world into existence through sacred words.

gods.name: Tupã
gods.description: Thunder-god; sent by Ñamandú to complete creation. In Christianized Guaraní, the word for 'God'.

gods.name: Kuarahy
gods.description: Sun — elder of the divine twins. Led his brother through trials to retrieve their mother's bones.

gods.name: Jasy
gods.description: Moon — younger twin. Often naive to his brother's elder wisdom.

gods.name: Jurupari
gods.description: Lawgiver of masculine ritual. Women who see his mysteries were traditionally killed.

gods.name: Ngenechen
gods.description: Ruler of the Wenumapu. Four-in-one: old-man, old-woman, young-man, young-woman — simultaneously.

gods.name: Elchen
gods.description: Creator of humans.

gods.name: Kai Kai Vilu
gods.description: Sea-serpent who tried to flood the world. Fought by Tren Tren Vilu, the earth-serpent, in a cosmic battle echoed in Andean flood myths.

gods.name: Tren Tren Vilu
gods.description: Land-serpent who raised mountains to save humanity from Kai Kai Vilu's flood.

gods.name: Wekufe
gods.description: General category of evil spirits controlled by kalku (malevolent shamans). Counterbalance to Ngenechen.

gods.name: The Monad
gods.description: The True God of Gnosticism. Unknowable, beyond being. Not the creator-god of Genesis (who is the Demiurge).

gods.name: Barbelo
gods.description: First thought / reflection of the Monad. Mother of the aeons. Sometimes called the 'Virginal Spirit'.

gods.name: Sophia
gods.description: Aeon of Wisdom. Her fall — conceiving without her male consort — produced the Demiurge and the material world. Her restoration is salvation.

gods.name: Yaldabaoth
gods.description: The Demiurge. Ignorant of the true God, believes himself supreme. Identified by Gnostics with the God of the Hebrew Bible — a view that horrified orthodox Christians.

gods.name: Christ (aeon)
gods.description: In Gnostic systems, an aeon who descends to awaken humanity to its divine spark trapped in matter. Not identical to the Christ of orthodox Christianity.

gods.name: The Archons
gods.description: Seven or twelve planetary rulers subordinate to the Demiurge. Block souls' ascent back to the Pleroma.

gods.name: Seth (Sethian)
gods.description: In Sethian Gnosticism, a celestial figure (not the biblical son of Adam directly) who preserves the spiritual seed across history.

gods.name: Autogenes
gods.description: 'Self-generated' — the Logos aeon emerging from the union of Monad and Barbelo.

gods.name: The Four Luminaries
gods.description: Harmozel, Oroiael, Daveithai, and Eleleth — four great angelic realms of the Pleroma.

gods.name: Pistis Sophia
gods.description: 'Faith-Wisdom' — aspect or lower form of Sophia in the Pistis Sophia text; weeps repentance from the 13th aeon.

gods.name: Ein Sof
gods.description: 'Without end' — the infinite, unknowable aspect of God prior to any emanation.

gods.name: Keter
gods.description: Crown — first Sefirah. Pure will; the point where the unknowable becomes knowable.

gods.name: Chokhmah
gods.description: Wisdom — second Sefirah. The flash of insight before it can be articulated. Father-principle.

gods.name: Binah
gods.description: Understanding — third Sefirah. The womb that gives form to insight. Mother-principle.

gods.name: Chesed
gods.description: Mercy — fourth Sefirah. Boundless giving. Expanding force.

gods.name: Gevurah
gods.description: Severity — fifth Sefirah. Restrains Chesed's boundless flow. The principle of limit.

gods.name: Tiferet
gods.description: Beauty — sixth Sefirah. Balance between mercy and severity. Central Sefirah of the tree.

gods.name: Netzach
gods.description: Victory — seventh Sefirah. Endurance and outward thrust.

gods.name: Hod
gods.description: Splendor — eighth Sefirah. Acknowledgment, surrender, and ritual form.

gods.name: Yesod
gods.description: Foundation — ninth Sefirah. The conduit through which upper Sefirot flow into manifestation.

gods.name: Malkhut / Shekhinah
gods.description: Kingdom — tenth Sefirah. God's immanent presence in the world (Shekhinah). Feminine divinity; in exile with Israel, reunited in final redemption.

gods.name: Metis
gods.description: Titaness of cunning; Zeus's first wife. He swallowed her to prevent a prophesied usurpation; she continued to counsel him from within.

gods.name: Maia
gods.description: Eldest of the Pleiades. Mother of Hermes by Zeus.

gods.name: Pandora
gods.description: First mortal woman, fashioned by Hephaestus. Opened a jar releasing all ills into the world — only Hope remained.

gods.name: Pyrrha
gods.description: Survived the Greek flood with Deucalion. Repopulated the earth by throwing stones behind her.

gods.name: Deucalion
gods.description: Greek Noah. Warned by Prometheus of Zeus's flood, he and Pyrrha built an ark.

gods.name: Clymene
gods.description: Oceanid; consort of Iapetus, mother of Prometheus and his brothers.

gods.name: Nereus
gods.description: Old Man of the Sea. Prophetic and truth-telling; father of the fifty Nereids.

gods.name: Phorcys
gods.description: Primordial sea-god; with Ceto, father of most of the dangerous sea-monsters.

gods.name: Ceto
gods.description: Primordial sea-goddess of monsters. With Phorcys, mother of the Gorgons, Graeae, and Echidna.

gods.name: Thaumas
gods.description: Sea-god of wonders. Father of Iris (the rainbow) and the Harpies.

gods.name: Amphitrite
gods.description: Queen of the sea; wife of Poseidon.

gods.name: Triton
gods.description: Fish-tailed sea-god. Blows his conch-shell to calm or stir the waves.

gods.name: Hebe
gods.description: Cupbearer of the gods before Ganymede. Married the deified Heracles on Olympus.

gods.name: Eileithyia
gods.description: Goddess of childbirth. Her presence was required for any delivery; Hera withheld her to delay the birth of Heracles.

gods.name: Harmonia
gods.description: Wife of Cadmus, founder of Thebes. Her cursed necklace brought tragedy to generations.

gods.name: Phobos
gods.description: Personified fear. Accompanied his father into battle.

gods.name: Deimos
gods.description: Personified terror. Mars's two moons are named Phobos and Deimos.

gods.name: Ariadne
gods.description: Cretan princess. Gave Theseus the thread to escape the Labyrinth; abandoned on Naxos, where Dionysus found and married her.

gods.name: Semele
gods.description: Mortal mother of Dionysus. Tricked by Hera into asking Zeus to appear in full divine form; incinerated. Later rescued from Hades.

gods.name: Europa
gods.description: Phoenician princess abducted by Zeus in the form of a white bull. Eponym of the continent.

gods.name: Leda
gods.description: Queen of Sparta. Seduced by Zeus in swan-form; bore two eggs containing Helen, Pollux, Castor, and Clytemnestra.

gods.name: Danaë
gods.description: Imprisoned by her father to thwart a prophecy; Zeus reached her as a shower of gold. Gave birth to Perseus.

gods.name: Alcmene
gods.description: Mother of Heracles. Zeus approached her in the guise of her husband for an extended wedding-night.

gods.name: Helen
gods.description: Most beautiful woman in the world; her abduction by Paris ignited the Trojan War.

gods.name: Minos
gods.description: Legendary king of Crete; after death, a judge of the underworld.

gods.name: Io
gods.description: Priestess of Hera loved by Zeus, turned into a cow to escape Hera's notice. Wandered the world tormented by a gadfly before regaining her form in Egypt.

gods.name: Psyche
gods.description: Mortal woman whose beauty enraged Aphrodite. Married Eros; completed impossible trials to reunite with him and was granted immortality.

gods.name: Andromeda
gods.description: Ethiopian princess chained to a rock as sacrifice to Cetus; rescued by Perseus. Ancestress of the Perseid line, including Heracles.

gods.name: Cerberus
gods.description: Three-headed hound guarding the underworld's gate.

gods.name: Lernaean Hydra
gods.description: Many-headed water-serpent. Each head regenerated two when cut. Slain by Heracles as his second labor.

gods.name: Chimera
gods.description: Lion-goat-serpent hybrid. Slain by Bellerophon from the back of Pegasus.

gods.name: Pegasus
gods.description: Winged horse born from Medusa's severed neck. Aid of Bellerophon; later carried Zeus's thunderbolts.

gods.name: Orion
gods.description: Giant hunter. Killed by Artemis (versions differ); placed among the stars with his dog Sirius.

gods.name: Aeneas
gods.description: Trojan prince, son of Aphrodite. Fled burning Troy; after long wanderings founded Roman civilization in Italy. Central to Virgil's Aeneid.

gods.name: Anchises
gods.description: Trojan noble loved by Aphrodite, father of Aeneas. Blinded/crippled by Zeus for boasting of the affair.

gods.name: Adonis
gods.description: Beautiful youth loved by Aphrodite and Persephone. Killed by a boar; his blood grew into anemones. Dying-and-rising god imported from Phoenician Adon.

gods.name: Circe
gods.description: Witch-goddess of Aeaea. Transformed Odysseus's crew into swine; later became his ally and lover.

gods.name: Jörd
