gods.description: Personified earth; mother of Thor by Odin.

gods.name: Bestla
gods.description: Jötunn mother of Odin and his brothers.

gods.name: Vili
gods.description: Brother of Odin. With Odin and Vé, slew Ymir and built the world.

gods.name: Vé
gods.description: Brother of Odin and Vili. Gave humans their senses and appearance.

gods.name: Angrboda
gods.description: Jötunn consort of Loki. Mother of his three monstrous children.

gods.name: Sigyn
gods.description: Loki's loyal Aesir wife. Holds a bowl catching serpent-venom that would otherwise drip on her bound husband.

gods.name: Forseti
gods.description: God of justice. His hall Glitnir was golden and silver.

gods.name: Nanna
gods.description: Wife of Baldr. Died of grief at his death and was placed on his funeral pyre.

gods.name: Mímir
gods.description: Ancient wise being. Guardian of the well beneath Yggdrasil; Odin sacrificed an eye to drink from it. Beheaded by the Vanir; Odin preserved his head for counsel.

gods.name: Aegir
gods.description: Jötunn of the sea; hosted feasts for the gods. Brewed ale in a great cauldron.

gods.name: Rán
gods.description: Sea-goddess who catches drowned sailors in her net.

gods.name: Höðr
gods.description: Blind god who killed Baldr with a mistletoe dart guided by Loki. Later killed by Vali in retribution.

gods.name: Víðarr
gods.description: Silent god fated to avenge Odin by killing Fenrir at Ragnarök. One of the few gods who survive.

gods.name: Váli
gods.description: Born for one purpose: to avenge Baldr by killing Höðr. Grew to adulthood in a single day.

gods.name: Valkyries
gods.description: Choosers of the slain; Odin's maidens who select warriors for Valhalla.

gods.name: Aditi
gods.description: Mother of the Adityas (solar deities). Primordial boundless space.

gods.name: Kashyapa
gods.description: Great rishi; ancestor of gods, demons, and many creatures via his many wives.

gods.name: Vayu
gods.description: Wind-god; father of Hanuman and Bhima.

gods.name: Ashvins
gods.description: Divine horseman-twins; healers of the gods. Direct cognates of the Dioscuri.

gods.name: Sita
gods.description: Incarnation of Lakshmi; wife of Rama. Born from the earth in a furrow. Abducted by Ravana; her faithfulness is central to the Ramayana.

gods.name: Radha
gods.description: Beloved of Krishna; in many traditions regarded as Krishna's female counterpart and the supreme divinity.

gods.name: Rati
gods.description: Goddess of erotic desire; consort of Kamadeva.

gods.name: Ganga
gods.description: Personification of the Ganges. Descended from heaven; Shiva caught her in his locks to soften her impact on the earth.

gods.name: Yami
gods.description: Twin-sister of Yama; the Yamuna River. In some Vedic hymns, propositions to her twin are refused — the moral boundary of incest.

gods.name: Manu
gods.description: First man; survived the great flood by heeding Vishnu's fish-avatar warning. Author of the Manusmriti.

gods.name: Karna
gods.description: Tragic warrior of the Mahabharata. Abandoned at birth by Kunti; raised by a charioteer; fought for the losing Kauravas against his unknown half-brothers.

gods.name: Saranyu
gods.description: Cloud-goddess; fled Surya's heat. Mother of Yama, Manu, and the Ashvins.

gods.name: Tvashtr
gods.description: Divine craftsman of the Vedas; father of Saranyu and (in some versions) the serpent Vritra.

gods.name: Seshat
gods.description: Goddess of writing; scribe and consort of Thoth. Recorded the length of each pharaoh's reign.

gods.name: Sobek
gods.description: Crocodile-headed god. Patron of the Nile's power; protector of pharaohs.

gods.name: Neith
gods.description: Ancient creator-goddess of Sais. Weaver of the world; sometimes mother of Ra.

gods.name: Khnum
gods.description: Ram-headed god who molded human children on his potter's wheel. Source of the Nile's annual flood.

gods.name: Bes
gods.description: Dwarf-god protector of households and childbirth. Grotesque features scared away evil spirits.

gods.name: Taweret
gods.description: Hippopotamus-goddess protector of pregnant women.

gods.name: Ammit
gods.description: 'Devourer' — part-crocodile, part-lion, part-hippopotamus. Ate the hearts of the unworthy dead at judgment.

gods.name: Wadjet
gods.description: Cobra-goddess of Lower Egypt; appears on pharaoh's uraeus crown.

gods.name: Nekhbet
gods.description: Vulture-goddess of Upper Egypt; paired with Wadjet as the 'Two Ladies'.

gods.name: Aten
gods.description: Solar disk. Elevated by Akhenaten (r. ~1353–1336 BCE) as the sole god — Egypt's brief monotheistic interlude, reversed after his death.

gods.name: Nammu
gods.description: Primeval Sumerian sea-goddess; engendered heaven (An) and earth (Ki). Mother of Enki.

gods.name: Ninlil
gods.description: Consort of Enlil; mother of Nanna. Followed Enlil to the underworld after his exile.

gods.name: Lahmu
gods.description: First generation of gods in the Enuma Elish; with Lahamu, parents of the cosmic horizon.

gods.name: Lahamu
gods.description: Female counterpart of Lahmu.

gods.name: Anshar
gods.description: 'Whole heaven' — primordial horizon. Father of Anu.

gods.name: Kishar
gods.description: 'Whole earth' — primordial horizon. Mother of Anu.

gods.name: Damgalnuna
gods.description: 'Great princess' — consort of Enki, mother of Marduk.

gods.name: Enkidu
gods.description: Wild man created by Aruru to rival Gilgamesh. Became his closest friend; his death set Gilgamesh on his quest for immortality.

gods.name: Ningal
gods.description: Moon-goddess consort of Nanna.

gods.name: Kingu
gods.description: Second consort of Tiamat; led her army. Marduk slew him and mixed his blood with clay to create humans.

gods.name: Sarpanit
gods.description: Consort of Marduk in Babylonian religion.

gods.name: Boann
gods.description: Goddess of the River Boyne. Approached Nechtan's forbidden well; it overflowed, drowning her and forming the river.

gods.name: Lir
gods.description: Sea-god; father of Manannán. In 'Children of Lir', his four children were cursed by jealous Aoife to spend 900 years as swans.

gods.name: Elatha
gods.description: Handsome Fomorian prince; father of Bres and (in some accounts) the Dagda himself.

gods.name: Bres
gods.description: Briefly king of the Tuatha Dé Danann after Nuada's maiming; oppressed his people; overthrown.

gods.name: Macha
gods.description: War-goddess; raced against Ulster's horses while pregnant and cursed the men of Ulster with labor-pains at need. One of the triple Morrígan.

gods.name: Badb
gods.description: Crow of battle; one of the triple Morrígan with Macha and Nemain (or the Morrígan herself).

gods.name: Epona
gods.description: Gallic horse-goddess. Uniquely for a Celtic deity, worshipped widely throughout the Roman Empire — patroness of cavalry.

gods.name: Cernunnos
gods.description: Antlered horned-god of the Gauls; depicted on the Gundestrup cauldron. Lord of wild nature and trade.

gods.name: Taranis
gods.description: Gallic thunder-god. Wheel-bearer. Equated by Romans with Jupiter.

gods.name: Centeotl
gods.description: Maize-god. Central to Aztec agricultural ritual.

gods.name: Ehecatl
gods.description: Wind-god; an aspect of Quetzalcoatl. Round temples were built for him because wind has no corners.

gods.name: Mayahuel
gods.description: Goddess of the maguey plant and pulque (fermented maguey sap). Many-breasted — nursed the 400 rabbit-gods of drunkenness.

gods.name: Tonantzin
gods.description: 'Our revered mother'. Associated with Tepeyac, where the apparition of Our Lady of Guadalupe was later reported in 1531.

gods.name: Mictecacíhuatl
gods.description: Queen of Mictlan. Her festival persists in modern Day of the Dead observances.

gods.name: Xolotl
gods.description: Dog-headed god; twin of Quetzalcoatl. Guides the sun through the underworld and escorts souls of the dead.

gods.name: Aether
gods.description: Pure upper atmosphere breathed by the gods.

gods.name: Hemera
gods.description: Personified day. Her mother Nyx left the sky as she arrived.

gods.name: Eurybia
gods.description: Sea-titaness of mastery over sea-power. Consort of the Titan Crius.

gods.name: Astraeus
gods.description: Titan of dusk; father of the winds and planets by Eos.

gods.name: Samael
gods.description: Ambiguous angel — both accuser and angel of death. Consort of Lilith in medieval Kabbalistic lore.

gods.name: Azazel
gods.description: Desert-figure to whom the Yom Kippur scapegoat was sent. In Enochic literature, a leader of fallen Watchers.

gods.name: Behemoth
gods.description: Primordial land-creature of Job 40. Paired in apocalyptic tradition with the sea-monster Leviathan.

gods.name: Leviathan
gods.description: Chaos-serpent of Job and the Psalms. Reflex of Canaanite Yam/Lotan. Eschatological banquet-food in rabbinic tradition.

gods.name: Perses
gods.description: Titan of destruction; husband of Asteria, father of Hecate.

gods.name: Asteria
gods.description: Titaness of falling stars; leapt into the sea to escape Zeus and became the island of Delos.

gods.name: Pallas
gods.description: Titan of battle; consort of Styx. Not to be confused with Pallas Athena.

gods.name: Caelus
gods.description: Roman personified sky; Uranus in Latin dress.

gods.name: Tellus
gods.description: Roman earth-mother; also called Terra.

gods.name: Aurora
gods.description: Roman dawn; Latin form of Eos.

gods.name: Proserpina
gods.description: Roman Persephone; queen of the underworld.

gods.name: Cupid
gods.description: Roman Eros. Winged archer whose arrows compel love.

gods.name: Sol
gods.description: Roman sun-god. Later elevated as Sol Invictus, state-sponsored from the 3rd century CE until displaced by Christianity.

gods.name: Luna
gods.description: Roman moon-goddess.

gods.name: Rhea Silvia
gods.description: Legendary vestal virgin; raped by Mars, bore Romulus and Remus.

gods.name: Remus
gods.description: Twin brother of Romulus. Killed by him over the naming of Rome.

gods.name: Latona
gods.description: Roman form of Leto; mother of Apollo and Diana.

gods.name: Fortuna
gods.description: Goddess of luck. Blindfolded; turns the wheel of fortune. Oracle at Praeneste.

gods.name: Bona Dea
gods.description: 'Good Goddess'. Rites performed only by women; males were strictly forbidden.

gods.name: Cybele
gods.description: Anatolian mother-goddess imported to Rome in 204 BCE. Her ecstatic rites included self-castrated priests (Galli).

gods.name: Attis
gods.description: Consort of Cybele. Castrated himself in madness; died and revived. Phrygian-origin dying-and-rising god.

gods.name: Mithras
gods.description: Roman mystery-god derived (loosely) from Iranian Mithra. Popular among Roman soldiers; worshipped in underground mithraea. Tauroctony (bull-slaying) is the core icon.

gods.name: Dioscuri
gods.description: Castor and Pollux — divine twin horsemen. One mortal (son of Tyndareus), one immortal (son of Zeus); Pollux shared his immortality. Constellation Gemini.

gods.name: Coronis
gods.description: Mother of Asclepius. Killed by Apollo for infidelity; her son cut from her corpse.

gods.name: Electryon
gods.description: King of Tiryns; son of Perseus, grandfather of Heracles.

gods.name: Medusa
gods.description: Only mortal of the three Gorgons. Slain by Perseus; her severed head retained its petrifying gaze.

gods.name: Melinoe
gods.description: Chthonic goddess of ghosts. Orphic tradition.

gods.name: Zagreus
gods.description: Orphic 'first Dionysus'. Dismembered by Titans; humanity arose from their ashes, carrying both Titan evil and Dionysian divinity.

gods.name: Hermaphroditus
gods.description: Merged with the nymph Salmacis into a single dual-sexed being. Eponym of hermaphrodite.

gods.name: Priapus
gods.description: Phallic fertility god; protector of gardens. Perpetually and grotesquely aroused.

gods.name: Hedone
gods.description: Personified pleasure. Source of the word 'hedonism'.

gods.name: Anemoi
gods.description: The four wind-gods: Boreas (north), Notus (south), Eurus (east), Zephyrus (west).

gods.name: Astra Planeta
gods.description: Five gods of the visible planets: Phainon (Saturn), Phaethon (Jupiter), Pyroeis (Mars), Eosphoros (Venus), Stilbon (Mercury).

gods.name: Electra
gods.description: Oceanid consort of Thaumas.

gods.name: Pleione
gods.description: Oceanid mother of the Pleiades.

gods.name: Perse
gods.description: Oceanid consort of Helios.

gods.name: Aeetes
gods.description: King of Colchis; keeper of the Golden Fleece. Father of Medea.

gods.name: Pasiphae
gods.description: Wife of Minos. Cursed by Poseidon to love a bull; mother of the Minotaur.

gods.name: Minotaur
gods.description: Bull-headed man confined in Daedalus's Labyrinth; fed on Athenian youths until slain by Theseus.

gods.name: Cyclopes
gods.description: One-eyed giants. Forged Zeus's thunderbolts, Poseidon's trident, Hades's helm.

gods.name: Hecatoncheires
gods.description: Hundred-handed fifty-headed giants. Imprisoned in Tartarus by Uranus, freed by Zeus in the Titanomachy.

gods.name: Oceanids
gods.description: Three thousand nymph-daughters of Oceanus and Tethys; presided over bodies of water.

gods.name: Nereids
gods.description: Fifty sea-nymph daughters of Nereus and Doris.

gods.name: Horae
gods.description: Goddesses of seasons and social order: Eunomia (good order), Dike (justice), Eirene (peace).

gods.name: Graces
gods.description: Three goddesses — Aglaea, Euphrosyne, Thalia. Attendants of Aphrodite; personify beauty, mirth, and good cheer.

gods.name: Menoetius
gods.description: Titan of violent rage. Struck down by Zeus in the Titanomachy.

gods.name: Pleiades
gods.description: Seven nymph-sisters: Maia, Alcyone, Celaeno, Electra, Sterope, Taygete, Merope. Placed among the stars as the Pleiades cluster.

gods.name: Daphne
gods.description: Naiad pursued by Apollo; transformed into a laurel tree to escape him.

gods.name: Sphinx
gods.description: Winged lion-woman who terrorized Thebes with her riddle. Destroyed herself when Oedipus solved it.

gods.name: Nemean Lion
gods.description: Invulnerable lion strangled by Heracles in his first labor. Wore its skin thereafter.

gods.name: Ladon
gods.description: Hundred-headed serpent guarding the golden apples of the Hesperides.

gods.name: Chrysaor
gods.description: Giant-warrior born from Medusa's neck alongside Pegasus.

gods.name: Nerthus
gods.description: Germanic earth-goddess described by Tacitus. May be sister-consort of Njörd.

gods.name: Fjörgyn
gods.description: Earth-goddess. Name is cognate with Baltic Perkūnas (PIE *Perkʷunos) — here a feminine earth, indicating the PIE pair was originally both.

gods.name: Nótt
gods.description: Personified night. Drives a horse-drawn chariot ahead of Dagr (day).

gods.name: Norns
gods.description: Urd (past), Verdandi (present), Skuld (future). Weave the fates of men and gods beside the well at Yggdrasil's roots.

gods.name: Gunnlöð
gods.description: Jötunn maiden; guardian of the mead of poetry. Seduced by Odin, who stole the mead.

gods.name: Rindr
gods.description: Mother of Váli by Odin — conceived to bear Baldr's avenger.

gods.name: Gríðr
gods.description: Friendly Jötunn; mother of Víðarr; lent Thor her staff and belt of strength.

gods.name: Asha
gods.description: One of the Amesha Spentas. 'Truth' and cosmic order.

gods.name: Amesha Spentas
gods.description: Six (or seven, with Spenta Mainyu) 'Bounteous Immortals' — personified aspects of Ahura Mazda: Vohu Manah, Asha, Khshathra, Armaiti, Haurvatat, Ameretat.

gods.name: Armaiti
gods.description: Amesha Spenta of devotion and the earth. Consort/daughter of Ahura Mazda.

gods.name: Horus the Elder
gods.description: Original sky-falcon Horus, sibling of Osiris. Later merged with Horus son of Isis.

gods.name: Nefertem
gods.description: Lotus-god; son of Ptah and Sekhmet. Dawn emerging from the primeval lotus.

gods.name: Naunet
gods.description: Female counterpart of Nun in the Hermopolitan Ogdoad.

gods.name: Four Sons of Horus
gods.description: Imsety, Hapi, Duamutef, and Qebehsenuef — guardians of the canopic jars holding organs of the mummified dead.

gods.name: Beli
gods.description: Welsh ancestral king; consort of Dôn. Founder-figure of Welsh royal genealogies.

gods.name: Manawydan
gods.description: Welsh sea-god. Cognate with Irish Manannán. Married Rhiannon after Pwyll's death.

gods.name: Llŷr
gods.description: Welsh sea-god. Cognate with Irish Lir; father of Brân, Branwen, and Manawydan.

gods.name: Tekawerahkwa
gods.description: Daughter of Sky-Woman; mother of the Twins. Died giving birth to Flint, who clawed out her side.

gods.name: Anansi
gods.description: Spider-trickster of the Akan and broader West African traditions. Folk tales traveled to the Americas; became the trickster of Caribbean and African-American folklore.

gods.name: Kotoamatsukami
gods.description: The 'separate heavenly deities' — first five kami who appeared at the beginning.

gods.name: El (Semitic supreme)
gods.description: Semitic root-word for 'god'. Cognate across Canaanite El, early Israelite Elohim, Arabian Il, and later Allāh.

gods.name: Lugalbanda
gods.description: Legendary king of Uruk and second king of the Sumerian King List's post-flood dynasty. Deified; father of Gilgamesh.

gods.name: Ninsun
gods.description: 'Wild Cow' goddess; mother of Gilgamesh. Counseled her son through the Epic.

gods.name: Phaedra
gods.description: Sister of Ariadne, wife of Theseus. Fell fatally in love with her stepson Hippolytus — subject of tragedies by Euripides, Seneca, and Racine.

gods.name: Doris
gods.description: Oceanid consort of Nereus; mother of the fifty Nereids.

gods.name: Styx
gods.description: Oceanid river-goddess of the underworld. The gods swore unbreakable oaths on her waters.

gods.name: Tithonus
gods.description: Trojan prince loved by Eos. Granted immortality but not eternal youth; shriveled into a cicada.

gods.name: Memnon
gods.description: King of Ethiopia who fought for Troy; slain by Achilles. Eos won posthumous honors for him from Zeus.

gods.name: Orthrus
gods.description: Two-headed hound of Geryon; slain by Heracles.

gods.name: Alcaeus
gods.description: Son of Perseus. Grandfather of Heracles through Amphitryon.

gods.name: Dryope
gods.description: Oread/wood-nymph; mother of Pan by Hermes. Transformed into a lotus-tree.

gods.name: Alcis
gods.description: Divine twin-gods of the Naharvali (Germanic) worshipped in a sacred grove, per Tacitus. Direct continuation of the PIE Divine Twins.

gods.name: Parcae
gods.description: Roman Fates: Nona, Decima, Morta. Cognate roles to Greek Moirai.

gods.name: Larunda
gods.description: Silent nymph mother of the Lares; punished by Jupiter for gossip by having her tongue cut out.

gods.name: Latinus
gods.description: Legendary king of the Latins; gave his daughter Lavinia in marriage to Aeneas.

gods.name: Picus
gods.description: Italian woodpecker-god of augury. Transformed by Circe when he refused her.

gods.name: Sarruma
gods.description: Son of Teshub/Tarhunt and Hebat. Mountain-god, depicted riding a panther.

gods.name: Potamoi
gods.description: River-gods; three thousand sons of Oceanus and Tethys. Each river had its own Potamos.

gods.name: Pachacamac
gods.description: Coastal creator-god of pre-Inca Peru, later absorbed into the Inca pantheon. His oracle on the central coast was venerated across the Andes.

gods.name: Mama Cocha
gods.description: Mother Sea. Protectress of coastal peoples and fishers.

gods.name: Manco Capac
gods.description: First Sapa Inca, son of Inti. Led his people to found Cusco by plunging a golden staff into fertile ground.

gods.name: Illapa
gods.description: Thunder-god. Prayed to for rain during drought; his sling-crack produces lightning.

gods.name: Haumea
gods.description: Great Hawaiian mother-goddess. Could renew her youth and give birth from many parts of her body.

gods.name: Kāne
gods.description: Greatest of the four main Hawaiian gods. Creator of the first man (Wakea) and source of fresh-water springs.

gods.name: Lono
gods.description: God of peace, agriculture, and the Makahiki harvest festival. Captain Cook was killed after being mistaken for (and then doubted as) Lono returning.

gods.name: Kū
gods.description: War-god of the Hawaiian four. Kamehameha's personal patron; human sacrifices were offered at his heiau.

gods.name: Svantovit
gods.description: Chief god of the West Slavs at the great temple of Arkona on Rügen. Four-headed; worshipped until Christian destruction of his idol in 1168.

gods.name: Stribog
gods.description: Wind-god of Vladimir's pantheon; ancestor of the winds. Mentioned in The Tale of Igor's Campaign.

gods.name: Khors
gods.description: Sun-disk god in Vladimir's pantheon. Name possibly borrowed from Iranian Xwaršēd.

gods.name: Ilmatar
gods.description: Maiden of the air. Descended to the primordial sea; a teal laid eggs on her knee that cracked to form the cosmos. Mother of Väinämöinen.

gods.name: Mielikki
gods.description: Forest-goddess; wife of Tapio. Hunters sought her favor for successful hunts.

gods.name: Tuonetar
gods.description: Queen of Tuonela, the Finnish underworld. Serves dark beer full of frogs and worms to the dead.

gods.name: Jowang
gods.description: Kitchen-goddess. A bowl of pure water on the hearth was her seat; she watched over the family's words.

gods.name: Chilseong
gods.description: Seven Stars — the Big Dipper deified. Prayed to for children and long life.

gods.name: Hunab Ku
gods.description: Sole God — a supreme creator-figure in post-conquest Maya sources; may be a Christian-influenced syncretism with older Itzamná theology.

gods.name: Yum Kaax
gods.description: Lord of the Forests. God of wild vegetation and the hunt. Protector of wild animals.

gods.name: Ah Puch
gods.description: Skeletal lord of the lowest level of Metnal (Maya underworld). Accompanied by owls, his messengers.

gods.name: Ixtab
gods.description: Rope Woman. Escorted the souls of those who hanged themselves directly to paradise — suicide was honorably regarded in certain contexts.

gods.name: Rainbow Serpent
gods.description: Widely shared creator-figure across Aboriginal Australia, known by many names (Ngalyod, Wagyl, Yingarna, Wollunqua). Shaped the landscape moving through it; lives in waterholes.

gods.name: Baiame
gods.description: Sky-father of the Kamilaroi, Wiradjuri, and other southeastern groups. Shaped mountains and rivers; gave ritual law.

gods.name: Bunjil
gods.description: Wedge-tailed eagle creator of the Kulin nations around Melbourne. Made the first humans from clay.

gods.name: Yhi
gods.description: Karraur sun-goddess. Awoke the sleeping Earth into the Dreaming; first of the Spirits.

gods.name: Bahloo
gods.description: Moon-man of the Kamilaroi. Wanders the night with his three dogs.

gods.name: Altjira
gods.description: Arrernte remote sky-father. Created the Earth then withdrew; the word Altjira also means 'Dreamtime' itself.

gods.name: Namarrgon
gods.description: 'Lightning Man' of the Kunwinjku (western Arnhem Land). Depicted in Kakadu rock art with stone axes on his head and elbows.

gods.name: Anjea
gods.description: Queensland fertility spirit. Placed infant souls in newborns at birth; returned them to clay mounds at death for the next cycle.

gods.name: Wawalag Sisters
gods.description: Two ancestral sisters of the Yolŋu. Swallowed by the python Yurlunggur — origin of initiation rites.

gods.name: Yúcahu
gods.description: Supreme male deity; god of manioc (the Taíno staple) and the sea. Mother-borne rather than fathered.

gods.name: Atabey
gods.description: Primordial mother-goddess. Self-generative; mother of Yúcahu. Five names across five aspects.

gods.name: Guabancex
gods.description: Storm-goddess; source of the word 'hurricane'. Attended by Guatauba (thunder) and Coatrisquie (rain).

gods.name: Boinayel
gods.description: Rain-god; his tears fertilize the land. Twin of Márohu.

gods.name: Márohu
gods.description: Dry-weather twin of Boinayel; the sunshine that follows the rain.

gods.name: Maketaori Guayaba
gods.description: Lord of Coaybay, the Taíno underworld. The opia (souls of the dead) dwelt there by day and emerged at night.

gods.name: Papa Legba
gods.description: Old man at the crossroads; first lwa invoked in every ceremony. Opens the gate between the human and spirit worlds. Derived from Fon Legba.

gods.name: Baron Samedi
gods.description: Top-hatted, sunglass-wearing lord of the Gede (spirits of the dead). Digs every grave; can refuse to accept a soul and return it to life. Both somber and lewd.

gods.name: Erzulie Freda
gods.description: Rada lwa of romantic love and luxury. Famous for her tears — she weeps because the world falls short of her vision of beauty.

gods.name: Damballa
gods.description: Great white serpent lwa; father of all lwa. Ancient and peaceful. Derived from Fon Dan.

gods.name: Ogou
gods.description: Lwa of iron, war, and revolution. Invoked in the 1791 Bois Caïman ceremony that sparked the Haitian Revolution.

gods.name: Maman Brigitte
gods.description: Fierce wife of Baron Samedi. Possibly derived from Irish Brigid brought by Celtic indentured servants to the Caribbean.

gods.name: Raðien
gods.description: Remote supreme god; delegated action to other deities. The highest of the Sámi upper-world.

gods.name: Beaivi
gods.description: Sun-mother. Brought reindeer to her people; her return after the polar night was marked by offerings of white animals.

gods.name: Horagalles
gods.description: Thunder-god. Name likely borrowed from 'Old Man Thor' (Tor karl). Wields a stone hammer.

gods.name: Máttaráhkká
gods.description: Great ancestress. Her three daughters each oversee a phase of human development: Sáráhkká (birth), Juoksáhkká (boys), Uksáhkká (door/protection).

gods.name: Leib-olmai
gods.description: 'Alder-man' — master of bears. Bear-hunters sprinkled themselves with alder-bark juice in his honor.

gods.name: Sabazios
gods.description: Thracian-Phrygian sky/horseman god. Identified by Greeks sometimes with Zeus, sometimes with Dionysus. His ecstatic cult reached Athens.

gods.name: Bendis
gods.description: Huntress-goddess. Her cult was officially adopted in Athens in 429 BCE (the Bendideia festival).

gods.name: Zalmoxis
gods.description: Getic religious founder said by Herodotus to have been a slave of Pythagoras. Taught his people that they would not die but go to live with him; disappeared into an underground chamber for three years, then reappeared.

gods.name: Kotys
gods.description: Goddess of ecstatic rites. Her mysteries spread to Corinth and became bywords for excess.

gods.name: Darzalas
gods.description: 'Great God' of Odessos (modern Varna). Chthonic; widely depicted on Black Sea coinage.

gods.name: Thetis
gods.description: Sea-nymph whom Zeus and Poseidon both desired — until a prophecy said her son would surpass his father. Married off to mortal Peleus; bore Achilles.

gods.name: Achilles
gods.description: Greatest Greek warrior at Troy. Dipped in the Styx as an infant — invulnerable except for the heel his mother held him by.

gods.name: Odysseus
gods.description: King of Ithaca; devised the Trojan Horse. Took ten years to return home — subject of the Odyssey.

gods.name: Jason
gods.description: Leader of the Argonauts; retrieved the Golden Fleece with Medea's help. Later abandoned her; died alone beneath the rotting Argo.

gods.name: Medea
gods.description: Colchian sorceress; granddaughter of Helios via Aeetes. Helped Jason win the Fleece; when abandoned, killed their children in vengeance.

gods.name: Orpheus
gods.description: Divine musician. Descended to retrieve his dead bride Eurydice; lost her by looking back. Orphic mystery religion traced itself to him.

gods.name: Cadmus
gods.description: Phoenician prince who founded Thebes. Sowed dragon's teeth that grew into warriors; brought writing to Greece.

gods.name: Bhudevi
gods.description: Earth-goddess; second consort of Vishnu. Rescued from the flood by the boar-avatar Varaha.

gods.name: Soma
gods.description: Personified sacred drink of the Vedic sacrifice. Merged with Chandra (moon) in later tradition. Cognate with Iranian Haoma.

gods.name: Rudra
gods.description: Vedic storm/archer god; feared for his arrows of disease. Evolved into Shiva.

gods.name: Kubera
gods.description: Plump lord of treasures; king of the yakshas. Rules from Alaka in the Himalayas; guardian of the north.

gods.name: Balarama
gods.description: Elder brother of Krishna; avatar of Vishnu's serpent Shesha. Wields a plow; sometimes counted as an avatar in his own right.

gods.name: Sif
gods.description: Wife of Thor; famous for her golden hair, which Loki cut off. The dwarves forged replacement hair of real gold.

gods.name: Ullr
gods.description: Winter god of skiers and archers. Named in more Scandinavian place-names than almost any other god — suggesting a bygone major cult.

gods.name: Kvasir
gods.description: Wisest being, born from the spittle of Aesir and Vanir ending their war. Murdered by dwarves, who brewed the mead of poetry from his blood.

gods.name: Gefjon
gods.description: Plowed away the island of Zealand from Sweden with four oxen — her sons by a giant. Sometimes counted among the Vanir.

gods.name: Eir
gods.description: Goddess of healing. May be counted as a Valkyrie, an Asynja, or a handmaiden of Frigg — sources disagree.

gods.name: Hapi
gods.description: God of the Nile's annual inundation. Depicted as androgynous, with a full belly and pendulous breasts — giver of abundance.

gods.name: Khepri
gods.description: Scarab-headed morning sun; aspect of Ra. Rolled the sun-disk across the sky like the dung-beetle rolls its ball.

gods.name: Satet
gods.description: Goddess of Aswan cataract — where the Nile was believed to emerge from the underworld. Consort of Khnum.

gods.name: Tlazolteotl
gods.description: 'Filth-eater'. Consumed confessions of sexual misdeeds, transforming sin to purity. Patron of midwives.

gods.name: Metztli
gods.description: Moon-goddess; often identified with Coyolxauhqui or as a separate aspect.

gods.name: Arawn
gods.description: King of Annwn (the Welsh otherworld). Traded realms for a year with Pwyll; his wife remained faithful to Arawn in his form.

gods.name: Math fab Mathonwy
gods.description: Magician-king of Gwynedd in the Fourth Branch. Unless at war, had to keep his feet in the lap of a virgin. Created Blodeuwedd from flowers with Gwydion.

gods.name: Orunmila
gods.description: Orisha of divination. Witnessed creation and knows the destiny of every soul. The Ifá oracle belongs to him.

gods.name: Olokun
gods.description: Orisha of the ocean depths. Gender-variable across traditions. Shackled on the ocean floor to prevent flooding the world.

gods.name: Agreskwe
gods.description: Huron war and hunting god. Prayed to before battle and the fall hunt.

gods.name: Hahgwehdiyu
gods.description: Alternate name for the Good Twin (Sapling) in some Seneca traditions.

gods.name: Mamlambo
gods.description: River-serpent goddess. Grants wealth to those who serve her — but demands blood in exchange.

gods.name: Inyanga
gods.description: Moon-goddess. The word 'inyanga' also means 'month' and 'traditional healer'.

gods.name: Shahar
gods.description: 'Dawn' — twin of Shalim. Both gods are likely reflected in the biblical Morning and Evening.

gods.name: Shalim
gods.description: 'Dusk' — twin of Shahar. His name survives in Jerusalem ('Yeru-Shalim', city of Shalim).

gods.name: Kothar-wa-Khasis
gods.description: Divine craftsman. Forged Baal's mace; his name means 'Skillful-and-Wise'.

gods.name: Aruru
gods.description: Mother-goddess of birth. Shaped the first humans from clay; later created Enkidu as a rival to Gilgamesh.

gods.name: Siduri
gods.description: Alewife at the edge of the world. Advised Gilgamesh to accept mortality and enjoy life.

gods.name: Wenchang
gods.description: Stellar god of literature; patron of scholars preparing for imperial examinations.

gods.name: Zhinu
gods.description: Weaver-girl (the star Vega). Separated from her mortal husband Niulang by the Milky Way; reunited once a year on Qixi.

gods.name: Tudi Gong
gods.description: 'Earth grandfather' — a local earth-god with a small shrine in each neighborhood. Part of the celestial bureaucracy's lowest tier.

gods.name: Benzaiten
gods.description: Goddess of all that flows — water, music, words, money. Japanese form of Indian Saraswati. One of the Seven Lucky Gods.

gods.name: Jizō
gods.description: Bodhisattva-kami hybrid. Protector of travelers, children, and souls of the unborn. Small stone statues of him line roads and cemeteries.

gods.name: Mama Ocllo
gods.description: Sister-wife of Manco Cápac; first Coya (queen) of the Incas. Taught Andean women to weave.

gods.name: Calliope
gods.description: Eldest of the Muses. Muse of epic poetry; mother of Orpheus.

gods.name: Calypso
gods.description: Sea-nymph who held Odysseus captive on Ogygia for seven years, offering him immortality. Released him at the gods command.

gods.name: Demophon
gods.description: Athenian king, son of Theseus. Broke his promise to return to the Thracian princess Phyllis, who died of grief.

gods.name: Tyche
gods.description: Greek goddess of fortune and the patron of cities. Often depicted with a mural crown and cornucopia.

gods.name: Purusha
gods.description: Vedic primal man. His self-sacrifice and dismemberment produced the universe and the four varnas — parallel to Ymir and Pangu.

gods.name: Þrúðr
gods.description: Daughter of Thor and Sif. Name means strength. Counted among the Valkyries in some sources.

gods.name: Narfi
gods.description: Son of Loki and Sigyn. Disemboweled by his transformed brother Vali to produce the bonds that chain Loki until Ragnarök.

gods.name: Váli (son of Loki)
gods.description: Son of Loki transformed by the Aesir into a wolf as part of Loki's punishment. Distinct from Odin's son Váli.

gods.name: Marichi
gods.description: One of the seven great rishis (Saptarishi) born from Brahma's mind. Father of Kashyapa, grandfather of the Devas.

gods.name: Atri
gods.description: One of the Saptarishi. Father of the moon-god Chandra and the sage Durvasa.

gods.name: Himavan
gods.description: Personified king of the Himalaya mountains. Father of Parvati (who married Shiva) and Ganga.

gods.name: Anjana
gods.description: Apsara cursed to earthly form. Conceived Hanuman by the wind-god Vayu.

gods.name: Lava
gods.description: Elder twin son of Rama and Sita, born in Valmikis hermitage after Sitas exile.

gods.name: Kusha
gods.description: Younger twin of Lava. Ancestor of the Kushvansha dynasty.

gods.name: Vasudeva
gods.description: Earthly father of Krishna. Carried the infant Krishna across the Yamuna to safety from Kamsa.

gods.name: Devaki
gods.description: Mother of Krishna. Imprisoned by her brother Kamsa after a prophecy that her eighth son would kill him.

gods.name: Ninsar
gods.description: Goddess of plants, born from Enki and Ninhursag after a nine-day divine gestation. Part of a four-generation Enki-daughter chain.

gods.name: Ninkurra
gods.description: Pasture-goddess, daughter-granddaughter of Enki via Ninsar.

gods.name: Uttu
gods.description: Weaver-goddess, last of the Enki-daughter chain. Advised by Ninhursag to refuse Enki's advances, setting off his eating of the forbidden plants.

gods.name: Cermait
gods.description: Son of the Dagda, father of the last three Tuatha Dé Danann kings of Ireland.

gods.name: Ernmas
gods.description: Mother-figure of Tuatha Dé Danann genealogies. Mother of the war triad (Morrígan, Badb, Macha) and the three sovereignty-goddesses of Ireland.

gods.name: Ruadán
gods.description: Son of Bres and Brigid. Killed attempting to assassinate Goibniu the smith at the second Battle of Moytura. Brigids lament over him invented keening.

gods.name: Dylan Ail Don
gods.description: Sea-being twin of Lleu in the Fourth Branch of the Mabinogi. Plunged into the sea at birth and swam like a fish.

gods.name: Pwyll
gods.description: Prince of Dyfed and hero of the First Branch of the Mabinogi. Swapped realms with Arawn of Annwn for a year; married Rhiannon.

gods.name: Pohjan neiti
gods.description: Daughter of Louhi. The beauty whose hand both Väinämöinen and Ilmarinen sought; Ilmarinen won her by forging the Sampo.

gods.name: Sedna
gods.description: Sea-mother. Thrown from her father's boat; her severed fingers became the whales, seals, and walruses. Angakkuit (shamans) descend to comb her hair to free marine animals for hunters.

gods.name: Anguta
gods.description: Psychopomp who gathers the souls of the dead and leads them to Adlivun, the underworld beneath the ocean.

gods.name: Nanook
gods.description: Master of bears; decided whether hunters deserved success. Observed tabus were essential — he took personal offence at breaches.

gods.name: Pinga
gods.description: Caretaker of living things. Received the souls of the dead to wait for reincarnation.

gods.name: Tekkeitsertok
gods.description: Master of caribou; received offerings from land-hunters.

gods.name: Silla
gods.description: Cosmic breath pervading all living things. When silla leaves a body, death comes. Not anthropomorphized.

gods.name: Malina
gods.description: Sun-goddess. Pursued through the sky by her brother Igaluk (moon) after an incestuous encounter in the dark.

gods.name: Igaluk
gods.description: Moon-man eternally chasing his sister Malina across the sky.

gods.name: Nyame
gods.description: Supreme sky-god of the Akan. Creator, remote but ultimate; delegates daily affairs to the abosom (lesser gods).

gods.name: Asase Yaa
gods.description: Earth-mother; wife of Nyame. Oaths were sworn by her; Thursday is her day (no farming).

gods.name: Kwaku Anansi
gods.description: Son of Nyame. Bought all the world's stories from his father. Ancestor of storytelling itself; survived slavery as Aunt Nancy and Brer Rabbit.

gods.name: Bia
gods.description: River-spirit god. Tricked out of prime lands by his brother Tano, leaving Bia with dense forests.

gods.name: Tano
gods.description: River-god of the Tano river. Older brother Bia's rival. Second-most powerful after Nyame.

gods.name: Owuo
gods.description: Death personified. Rules the afterlife; all Akan eventually come to him.

gods.name: Bathala
gods.description: Supreme Tagalog god. Created the first man and woman; ruler of gods and mortals before Spanish missionaries replaced his name with the Christian God in Tagalog translations.

gods.name: Apolaki
gods.description: Sun-god. Fought his sister Mayari for rulership after Bathala's departure; lost an eye. They agreed to share — he rules day, she rules night.

gods.name: Mayari
gods.description: One-eyed moon-goddess. Split rulership with her brother Apolaki after their duel.

gods.name: Tala
gods.description: Star-goddess. Third sibling; brighter appearances of Venus as morning and evening star.

gods.name: Amihan
gods.description: Primordial bird. Struck the bamboo that produced the first humans, Malakas and Maganda.

gods.name: Lakapati
gods.description: Hermaphrodite fertility deity of the rice fields. Prayed to before planting; one of the kindest deities.

gods.name: Amanikable
gods.description: Sea-god. Angry and ill-tempered; drowned ships of those who failed to propitiate him. Rejected by a mortal he loved, Maganda.

gods.name: Father of Greatness
gods.description: Supreme deity of Light in Manichaean cosmology. Pre-eternal; dwells in the Paradise of Light. Emanates the aeons that battle Darkness.

gods.name: Mother of Life
gods.description: First emanation of the Father of Greatness. Brought forth Primal Man to battle Darkness.

gods.name: Primal Man
gods.description: First champion against the Prince of Darkness. Captured; his light was swallowed and dispersed into matter — the cosmic origin of the human soul's imprisonment.

gods.name: Living Spirit
gods.description: Second emanation. Rescued Primal Man and built the visible cosmos from the slain demons' corpses — its purpose is to gradually extract the trapped light.

gods.name: Jesus the Splendor
gods.description: Not the historical Jesus of orthodox Christianity, but a luminous aeon who awakens Adam to gnosis. Distinct from 'Jesus the Messiah' (a prophet in Manichaean scheme).

gods.name: Prince of Darkness
gods.description: Ruler of the Kingdom of Darkness, co-eternal with the Father of Greatness. Source of matter, desire, and all evil. Will be finally defeated when the last light-particle is extracted.

gods.name: Raven
gods.description: Great trickster-creator of the Pacific Northwest. Stole the sun, moon, and stars from the Sky Chief and released them into the world. Transforms between bird and man.

gods.name: Thunderbird
gods.description: Enormous cosmic bird. Wings beat thunder; eyes flash lightning. Carries whales from the sea in its talons.

gods.name: Nass-Shaki-Yeil
gods.description: Tlingit Sky Chief ('Old Raven at the Head of the Nass'). Kept the sun, moon, and stars in cedar boxes until Raven stole them.

gods.name: Komogwa
gods.description: Kwakwaka'wakw god of the undersea kingdom. Rules over submerged wealth — copper sheets were gifts from him.

gods.name: Dzunukwa
gods.description: Giant wild woman of the Kwakwaka'wakw woods. Snatches children but also bestows wealth — her cries give away her approach: 'Hu! Hu!'

gods.name: Mangala
gods.description: Personified Mars in the Navagraha. Red-skinned, weapon-armed; inauspicious placement brings martial misfortune.

gods.name: Budha
gods.description: Personified Mercury. Son of the moon-god Chandra. Not to be confused with the Buddha.

gods.name: Brihaspati
gods.description: Teacher of the Devas; personified Jupiter in the Navagraha. Spiritual counterweight to Shukra, teacher of the Asuras.

gods.name: Shukra
gods.description: Teacher of the Asuras; personified Venus. Knew the mantra of resurrection — kept his students returning to life through countless battles.

gods.name: Shani
gods.description: Saturn. Strict enforcer of karmic law; his gaze can destroy, but his favor brings deep discipline.

gods.name: Rahu
gods.description: Severed head of the asura Svarbhanu. Swallows the sun and moon, causing eclipses. One of the nine grahas.

gods.name: Ketu
gods.description: Headless body of Svarbhanu. Paired with Rahu; together they complete the nine grahas.

gods.name: Nirriti
gods.description: Goddess of decay and misfortune; guardian of the southwest direction in the Dikpalas.

gods.name: Ravana
gods.description: Ten-headed Rakshasa king of Lanka. Kidnapping Sita precipitated the Ramayana war in which he was slain by Rama. A devoted Shiva-worshipper and accomplished scholar despite his villainy.

gods.name: Mahishasura
gods.description: Buffalo-demon who briefly conquered the three worlds. Slain by Durga across a nine-day battle — the basis of Navaratri.

gods.name: Hiranyakashipu
gods.description: Asura who obtained near-total invulnerability from Brahma. Slain by Vishnu's Narasimha avatar, who exploited every loophole of the boon.

gods.name: Prahlada
gods.description: Child devotee of Vishnu. His father Hiranyakashipu's efforts to kill him for apostasy all failed — until Vishnu appeared as Narasimha.

gods.name: Ebisu
gods.description: Laughing patron of fishermen and good luck. Only native Japanese member of the Seven Lucky Gods.

gods.name: Daikokuten
gods.description: Great black god of wealth and the kitchen. Derived from the Hindu Mahakala but thoroughly Japanized.

gods.name: Bishamonten
gods.description: Armored protector of warriors. One of the Seven Lucky Gods; guardian of the north.

gods.name: Kichijōten
gods.description: Goddess of beauty and fortune. Japanese form of Lakshmi.

gods.name: Fukurokuju
gods.description: Long-headed god of wisdom and long life. Once a Chinese Taoist sage.

gods.name: Jurōjin
gods.description: Old-man god of long life. Accompanied by a deer said to be 1,500 years old.

gods.name: Hotei
gods.description: Fat laughing monk. God of contentment; based on the Chinese Chan monk Budai. The Seven Lucky Gods' cheerful face.

gods.name: Anuket
gods.description: Gazelle-headed goddess of the Nile at Aswan. Third of the Elephantine triad with Khnum and Satet.

gods.name: Mafdet
gods.description: Feline goddess of executions. Older than Bastet; slayer of venomous creatures on behalf of the pharaoh.

gods.name: Renenutet
gods.description: Cobra-goddess of the harvest and of the 'true name' given to each newborn.

gods.name: Bennu
gods.description: Purple heron of creation and cyclical rebirth; Egyptian prototype of the Greek phoenix.

gods.name: Apis
gods.description: Sacred bull of Memphis; living incarnation of Ptah. Only one Apis lived at a time; when he died, he was mummified and a new bull found.

gods.name: Þrymr
gods.description: Jötunn king who stole Mjölnir. Thor (dressed as bride Freyja) killed him at the wedding feast and recovered the hammer.

gods.name: Surtr
gods.description: Fire-giant of Muspelheim. At Ragnarök he leads his sons against the gods and burns the world with his flaming sword.

gods.name: Suttungr
gods.description: Jötunn who obtained the mead of poetry as blood-price for his father. Odin seduced his daughter Gunnlöð and stole the mead.

gods.name: Hermóðr
gods.description: Brave messenger of Odin. Rode to Hel to plead for Baldr's return — Hel granted it on a condition that Loki sabotaged.

gods.name: Suttung's father Gillingr
gods.description: Jötunn father of Suttungr. Drowned by the dwarf brothers Fjalar and Galar; his son's blood-price was the mead of poetry.

gods.name: Namtar
gods.description: Vizier of Ereshkigal in the underworld. Brought disease and fate to mortals.

gods.name: Geshtinanna
gods.description: Sister of Dumuzi. Offered to spend half each year in the underworld so he could return for the other half — the seasonal cycle.

gods.name: Goibniu
gods.description: Divine smith of the Tuatha Dé Danann. Forged weapons that never missed, and brewed the ale of immortality.

gods.name: Dian Cécht
gods.description: God of healing and surgery. Forged Nuada's silver arm; later killed his own more talented son Miach in jealousy.

gods.name: Tecciztecatl
gods.description: Hesitant moon-god. At the world-creation bonfire, he flinched; the braver Nanahuatzin jumped first to become the sun.

gods.name: Centzon Totochtin
gods.description: The 400 rabbits — gods of drunkenness. Variously numbered excess — 'as drunk as 400 rabbits' is an Aztec idiom.

gods.name: Buluc Chabtan
gods.description: War-god of violent and sudden death. Accompanied by Ah Puch (underworld) in depictions of sacrifice.

gods.name: Rishabhanatha
gods.description: First of 24 Jain Tirthankaras. In Jain cosmology he discovered agriculture, law, and kingship before renouncing all; in later Hindu scripture he was absorbed as an avatar of Vishnu.

gods.name: Ik Onkar
gods.description: 'One God'. The formless Absolute of Sikhism. First words of the Guru Granth Sahib. Not anthropomorphic; neither masculine nor feminine.

gods.name: Guru Nanak
gods.description: 1469–1539. Founder of Sikhism. 'Na koi Hindu, na koi Musalman' — there is neither Hindu nor Muslim.

gods.name: Guru Angad
gods.description: 1504–1552. Codified the Gurmukhi script used to write Punjabi and the Guru Granth Sahib.

gods.name: Guru Amar Das
gods.description: 1479–1574. Institutionalized the free community kitchen (langar) — every visitor, regardless of caste, ate together before meeting the Guru.

gods.name: Guru Ram Das
gods.description: 1534–1581. Founded the city of Amritsar — site of the Golden Temple.

gods.name: Guru Arjan
gods.description: 1563–1606. Compiled the Adi Granth (first scripture). Tortured to death under Emperor Jahangir — Sikhism's first martyr.

gods.name: Guru Hargobind
gods.description: 1595–1644. Wore two swords — spiritual authority and temporal power (miri-piri). Response to his father's martyrdom.

gods.name: Guru Har Rai
gods.description: 1630–1661. Known for establishing free medical care; sheltered the Mughal prince Dara Shikoh.

gods.name: Guru Har Krishan
gods.description: 1656–1664. Became Guru at age 5; died of smallpox at 8 while tending to Delhi's plague sick.

gods.name: Guru Tegh Bahadur
gods.description: 1621–1675. Beheaded in Delhi for refusing to convert to Islam — defending Hindu Kashmiris' right to their faith.

gods.name: Guru Gobind Singh
gods.description: 1666–1708. Founded the Khalsa (1699). Lost all four sons to Mughal persecution. Declared the Guru Granth Sahib the eternal Guru after him.

gods.name: Lü Dongbin
gods.description: Leader of the Eight Immortals. Scholar-poet; carries a demon-slaying sword. Patron of barbers and the ill.

gods.name: Li Tieguai
gods.description: Crippled beggar with an iron crutch. Medicine gourd holds elixirs for the afflicted.

gods.name: Zhongli Quan
gods.description: Chief of the Eight Immortals. His magic fan revives the dead and transforms stones into gold.

gods.name: Lan Caihe
gods.description: Flower-basket-carrying minstrel of indeterminate gender and age. Patron of florists.

gods.name: Zhang Guolao
gods.description: Ancient hermit who rode his donkey backwards. Carries a bamboo drum-tube.

gods.name: He Xiangu
gods.description: Only regularly female Immortal. Carries a lotus; gained immortality after eating mica powder.

gods.name: Han Xiangzi
gods.description: Flute-playing Immortal. Music could cause flowers to bloom instantly. Patron of musicians.

gods.name: Cao Guojiu
gods.description: Emperor's brother-in-law turned Immortal. Carries a jade tablet; patron of actors.

gods.name: Yanluo Wang
gods.description: King Yama of Chinese folk religion — fifth and chief judge of the ten courts of hell. Buddhist borrowing from Hindu Yama.

gods.name: Taiyi Shangren
gods.description: 'Great Unity' — personified pole star, supreme deity in some Taoist schools.

gods.name: Vasistha
gods.description: One of the Saptarishi. Royal preceptor of the Raghu dynasty (including Rama).

gods.name: Vishvamitra
gods.description: Began as a king; through penance became a Brahmarishi — the only kshatriya to achieve the highest rank.

gods.name: Bhrigu
gods.description: Saptarishi. Father of Shukra (Venus). Author of the Bhrigu Samhita, foundational astrology text.

gods.name: Agastya
gods.description: Saptarishi who carried Vedic culture south. In one story, drank the entire ocean in his palm to reveal hidden demons.

gods.name: Dattatreya
gods.description: Three-headed sage-deity embodying Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva together. Cornerstone of several yoga traditions.

gods.name: Narada
gods.description: Wandering sage with a vina. Travels between all three worlds carrying news (and sometimes provocations).

gods.name: Gula
gods.description: Great goddess of healing. Accompanied by a dog; physicians prayed to her.

gods.name: Nisaba
gods.description: Goddess of writing, counting, and grain. Sumerian patron of scribes before Nabu displaced her.

gods.name: Pazuzu
gods.description: King of the wind demons. Paradoxically worn as an amulet to ward off the infant-killer Lamashtu.

gods.name: Lamashtu
gods.description: Demoness who killed infants and unborn children. Warded off by Pazuzu amulets.

gods.name: Ashnan
gods.description: Sumerian grain-goddess. Debate between Ashnan and Lahar (cattle) is a famous Sumerian wisdom text.

gods.name: Erinyes
gods.description: The Furies — Alecto, Tisiphone, Megaera. Born from the blood of castrated Uranus. Hounded kin-killers.

gods.name: Arachne
gods.description: Mortal weaver who challenged Athena. Her superior weaving earned her transformation into a spider.

gods.name: Scylla
gods.description: Six-headed sea-monster opposite Charybdis. Snatched six of Odysseus's men from his ship.

gods.name: Charybdis
gods.description: Enormous whirlpool opposite Scylla. Sailors had to choose which monster to face.

gods.name: Cassandra
gods.description: Trojan princess cursed by Apollo: she would see the future truly, but never be believed.

gods.name: Tantalus
gods.description: Served his own son Pelops to the gods in a test. Eternal punishment in Tartarus: food and water forever just out of reach.

gods.name: Sisyphus
gods.description: Tricked Death twice. In punishment, eternally rolls a boulder uphill that always slips back.

gods.name: Amenominakanushi
gods.description: First kami to come into being; stood alone in the high plain of heaven. Never directly worshipped historically — too abstract.

gods.name: Takamimusubi
gods.description: Second primordial kami; agent of 'productive' cosmic principle.

gods.name: Kamimusubi
gods.description: Third primordial kami; feminine counterpart to Takamimusubi's masculine productive principle.

gods.name: Heka
gods.description: Personification of magic itself — the life-force every Egyptian god and spell relied on. Existed before the gods.

gods.name: Sia
gods.description: Personified perception and mental understanding. Paired with Hu (utterance).

gods.name: Hu
gods.description: Personified divine speech — the word that brings into being. With Sia and Heka, one of the forces of creation.

gods.name: Wepwawet
gods.description: 'Opener of Ways'. Wolf-headed god who preceded the pharaoh in battle and the dead into the underworld.

gods.name: Huracán
gods.description: K'iche' Maya creator-storm god. His name is the root of Spanish huracán and English 'hurricane'. Destroyed the wooden-people age in a flood.

gods.name: Bacabs
gods.description: Four brothers who hold up the four corners of the sky — one for each cardinal direction.

gods.name: Zorya
gods.description: Three sister-goddesses of dawn, dusk, and midnight. Open and close heaven's gates each day; chain the apocalyptic hound Simargl to the pole star.

gods.name: Simargl
gods.description: Winged hound chained to the pole star. If he ever breaks free, the universe ends.

gods.name: Fjalar and Galar
gods.description: Dwarf brothers who murdered Kvasir and brewed the mead of poetry from his blood mixed with honey.

gods.name: Aurvandil
gods.description: Thor carried him across the Élivágar rivers in a basket; a frozen toe broke off and Thor threw it into the sky — the star Aurvandil's Toe.

gods.name: Mama Zara
gods.description: 'Corn mother' — personified maize, essential staple of the Andes. Maize effigies dressed in textiles.

gods.name: Tork Angegh
gods.description: Grotesquely ugly but immensely powerful mountain-hero of Armenia. Could break boulders with his hands.

gods.name: Turan
gods.description: Etruscan goddess of love and beauty. Source of Roman Venus; consorted with Atunis (Adonis).

gods.name: Tages
gods.description: Child-sage who sprang from a plowed furrow. Dictated the Etruscan Discipline — haruspicy, lightning-reading, and ritual.

gods.name: Clio
gods.description: Muse of history. Carries a scroll.

gods.name: Erato
gods.description: Muse of lyric and erotic poetry. Patron of wedding songs.

gods.name: Euterpe
gods.description: Muse of music and lyric poetry. Carries a double-flute.

gods.name: Melpomene
gods.description: Muse of tragedy. Carries the tragic mask.

gods.name: Polyhymnia
gods.description: Muse of sacred song and pantomime. Often veiled and pensive.

gods.name: Terpsichore
gods.description: Muse of dance. Carries a lyre and plectrum.

gods.name: Thalia (Muse)
gods.description: Muse of comedy. Carries the comic mask. Distinct from Thalia the Grace.

gods.name: Urania
gods.description: Muse of astronomy. Carries a globe and compass.

gods.name: Hector
gods.description: Firstborn Trojan prince and greatest Trojan warrior. Slain by Achilles after killing Patroclus; his body dragged behind Achilles's chariot.

gods.name: Priam
gods.description: Aged king of Troy. Went alone to Achilles's tent to ransom Hector's body. Killed at the altar when the city fell.

gods.name: Hecuba
gods.description: Queen of Troy. Bore nineteen children; most perished in the war. Ended as a slave of the Greeks.

gods.name: Paris
gods.description: Trojan prince who judged the golden apple contest in favor of Aphrodite, earning Helen as reward — and a ten-year siege for Troy.

gods.name: Menelaus
gods.description: Husband of Helen; his honor required the Trojan War. Reclaimed her after the fall of Troy.

gods.name: Agamemnon
gods.description: Supreme commander of the Greek army at Troy. Sacrificed his daughter Iphigenia for favorable winds. Murdered on homecoming by his wife Clytemnestra.

gods.name: Patroclus
gods.description: Beloved companion of Achilles. Wore his armor into battle; killed by Hector. His death brought Achilles back to the war.

gods.name: Ajax
gods.description: Second-mightiest Greek at Troy after Achilles. Killed himself after Achilles's armor was awarded to Odysseus instead of him.

gods.name: Penelope
gods.description: Wife of Odysseus. Waited twenty years, putting off suitors by weaving a shroud she unravelled every night.

gods.name: Telemachus
gods.description: Son of Odysseus. Came of age searching for his father; helped him slaughter the suitors on his return.

gods.name: Oedipus
gods.description: Fulfilled the prophecy he fled: killed his father Laius and married his mother Jocasta. Blinded himself in horror.

gods.name: Antigone
gods.description: Defied King Creon to bury her brother Polyneices. Entombed alive; hanged herself before rescue.

gods.name: Daedalus
gods.description: Master craftsman and architect of the Labyrinth. Built wings of feathers and wax to escape Crete with his son Icarus.

gods.name: Icarus
gods.description: Flew too close to the sun. The wax melted, the wings failed, and he drowned in the sea that bears his name.

gods.name: Pomona
gods.description: Purely Latin goddess of fruit trees and orchards — no Greek counterpart.

gods.name: Terminus
gods.description: God of boundary markers. So old that when the Capitoline was rebuilt, Jupiter allowed Terminus alone to remain — the Roman state's boundary was sacred.

gods.name: Montu
gods.description: Falcon-headed war-god of Thebes. Patron of pharaohs in battle before the New Kingdom elevated Amun.

gods.name: Min
gods.description: Ithyphallic fertility god of Coptos. Protector of caravan routes to the Red Sea.

gods.name: Heket
gods.description: Frog-goddess of childbirth. Gave the final breath of life to the clay bodies Khnum shaped.

gods.name: Sopdu
gods.description: Falcon-god of the eastern Sinai frontier. Guardian of the eastern desert and the approach to Punt.

gods.name: Níðhǫggr
gods.description: Dragon gnawing at the roots of Yggdrasil. Eats the corpses of oath-breakers and murderers.

gods.name: Ratatoskr
gods.description: Squirrel running up and down Yggdrasil, carrying insults between the eagle at the top and Níðhǫggr at the roots.

gods.name: Auðumbla
gods.description: Primordial cow formed from the rime of Ginnungagap. Her four rivers of milk fed Ymir; she licked Buri free from the ice.

gods.name: Huginn and Muninn
gods.description: Odin's two ravens — Thought and Memory. Fly out each day across the world and report back each evening.

gods.name: Gullinbursti
gods.description: Golden-bristled boar forged by the dwarves for Freyr. Faster than any horse, his bristles illuminate the darkness.

gods.name: Snotra
gods.description: Goddess of wise self-control. Her name became the Old Norse adjective for a 'wise, courteous' woman.

gods.name: Ninazu
gods.description: Chthonic healing-god. Father of Ningishzida; his serpent-staff is an ancestor of the medical caduceus.

gods.name: Gibil
gods.description: Sumerian fire-god. Called on to purify — his flame was invoked over defendants to determine truth.

gods.name: Geshtu-e
gods.description: Lesser god whose blood and divine intelligence were mixed with clay to create humans in the Atrahasis narrative.

gods.name: Garuda
gods.description: Enormous eagle-man who bears Vishnu. Sworn enemy of the nagas. National symbols of Indonesia and Thailand.

gods.name: Nandi
gods.description: Bull mount of Shiva. A Nandi statue reclines facing every Shiva sanctum; devotees whisper wishes into his ear.

gods.name: Shesha
gods.description: Thousand-headed cosmic serpent. Vishnu reclines on him between world-cycles; he supports the earth on one of his hoods.

gods.name: Vasuki
gods.description: Serpent-king used as the churning-rope at the Samudra Manthan. Worn by Shiva around his neck.

gods.name: Sugriva
gods.description: Monkey-king of Kishkindha. Allied with Rama to defeat his tyrannical brother Vali; his army searched for Sita.

gods.name: Jambavan
gods.description: Ancient bear-king, born from Brahma's yawn. Helped Rama against Ravana and later fought Krishna over the Syamantaka jewel.

gods.name: Kamsa
gods.description: Tyrant-king of Mathura who imprisoned his sister Devaki and killed her first six children. Slain by his eighth nephew, Krishna.

gods.name: Bhishma
gods.description: Eighth son of Ganga, uncle of the Kauravas and Pandavas. Took a vow of lifelong celibacy; died on a bed of arrows in the Mahabharata war.

gods.name: Draupadi
gods.description: Born of fire. Wife to all five Pandava brothers. Her public humiliation in the Kaurava court set off the Mahabharata war.

gods.name: Arjuna
gods.description: Greatest archer of his age. Recipient of the Bhagavad Gita from Krishna on the Kurukshetra battlefield.

gods.name: Yudhishthira
gods.description: Eldest Pandava. Son of Dharma himself. Could never lie — until the one crucial half-truth that killed Drona.

gods.name: Bhima
gods.description: Strongest of the Pandavas. Killed all 100 Kauravas in battle, fulfilling his vow after Draupadi's humiliation.

gods.name: Nakula
gods.description: Twin son of the Ashvins. Fourth Pandava. Master of horses and the sword.

gods.name: Sahadeva
gods.description: Twin of Nakula. Youngest Pandava. Knew all that had ever happened and ever would — but was cursed never to speak unasked.

gods.name: Sun Wukong
gods.description: Stone monkey who rebelled against heaven, accompanied the monk Xuanzang west, and attained Buddhahood. Star of Journey to the West.

gods.name: Nezha
gods.description: Child-warrior born as a ball of flesh. Died to save his parents from a Dragon King's vengeance; resurrected from lotus petals by his master.

gods.name: Erlang Shen
gods.description: Three-eyed warrior-god. His truth-seeing third eye can pierce any illusion. Accompanied by the celestial hound Xiaotian Quan.

gods.name: Bai Ze
gods.description: Horned lion-creature who appeared to the Yellow Emperor and dictated the names of 11,520 kinds of supernatural beings.

gods.name: Jiuqian Xuannü
gods.description: 'Mysterious Woman of the Nine Heavens'. Taught the Yellow Emperor the art of war; patron of magic and inner alchemy.

gods.name: Kannon
gods.description: Bodhisattva of compassion. Japanese form of Avalokiteśvara (via Chinese Guanyin). Worshipped at Sensō-ji in Tokyo and Kiyomizu-dera in Kyoto.

gods.name: Ame-no-Uzume
gods.description: Kami whose raucous dance (partly stripping) outside the Heavenly Rock Cave drew Amaterasu out and returned light to the world.

gods.name: Ninigi-no-Mikoto
gods.description: Grandson of Amaterasu. Descended from heaven to rule Japan; great-grandfather of the first emperor Jimmu.

gods.name: Konohanasakuya-hime
gods.description: Blossom-princess; wife of Ninigi. Her father's curse upon their rejection of her older sister made human life as short as cherry-blossom.

gods.name: Toyotama-hime
gods.description: Dragon-princess of the sea. Grandmother of the first emperor. Transformed into a sea-creature during childbirth; fled when her husband saw.

gods.name: Sukunabikona
gods.description: Dwarf-kami who arrived by the sea in a pod. Co-creator of Japan with Ōkuninushi; taught medicine and brewing.

gods.name: Belenus
gods.description: Gallic sun-god. Most widely attested Celtic deity via Roman-era dedications. May underlie the Beltane fire festival.

gods.name: Sucellus
gods.description: Bearded Gallic god with a long-handled hammer and an olla. 'The Good Striker'. Patron of agriculture and wine.

gods.name: Nantosuelta
gods.description: Gallic river and hearth goddess. Her name means 'winding-flowing'. Consort of Sucellus; often depicted with a raven and a house-on-a-pole.

gods.name: Rosmerta
gods.description: Great Gallic goddess of abundance. Under Roman rule, consort of Gaulish Mercury. 'Great Provider'.

gods.name: Patecatl
gods.description: 'Medicine Lord' — discoverer of peyote and pulque. Husband of Mayahuel.

gods.name: Chicomecoatl
gods.description: 'Seven Serpents' — goddess of maize and agricultural plenty. Female counterpart of Centeotl.

gods.name: Ek Chuaj
gods.description: Black-skinned god of merchants and cacao cultivation. Patron of travelers carrying their goods.

gods.name: Acan
gods.description: Mayan wine-god of the sacred fermented drink balché.

gods.name: Oba
gods.description: River-orisha; third wife of Shango. Cut off her own ear trying to win his love; the Oba river carries her grief.

gods.name: Iroko
gods.description: Spirit of the mighty iroko tree. Loggers make offerings before felling; the tree is considered haunted.

gods.name: Aganju
gods.description: Orisha of volcanoes, deserts, and untamed wilderness. In some traditions the father of Shango.

gods.name: Loko
gods.description: Tree spirit, knower of healing plants. Becomes the Haitian Vodou lwa Loco.

gods.name: Mami Wata
gods.description: Water-mother of West and Central African traditions — widespread across the diaspora. Holds a snake and a mirror; brings fortune and madness in equal measure.

gods.name: Agwe
gods.description: Lwa of the sea. Husband of La Sirene (mermaid-lwa). Offerings are cast to the sea in his honor.

gods.name: Kalfu
gods.description: Petwo counterpart of Papa Legba — rules the darker crossroads and unpredictable fortune.

gods.name: Marinette
gods.description: Fierce petwo lwa; owl-woman. Some traditions trace her to the mambo who sacrificed a pig at Bois Caïman to begin the Haitian Revolution.

gods.name: Rongo
gods.description: Maori god of peace and cultivated foods — especially kumara (sweet potato). Offerings of first fruits go to him.

gods.name: Kanaloa
gods.description: One of the four great Hawaiian gods. Often paired with Kāne. Ocean-mastery and the healing arts.

gods.name: Hiʻiaka
gods.description: Youngest sister of Pele. Patroness of hula. Carried her sleeping sister in an egg from Tahiti to Hawaii.

gods.name: Eingana
gods.description: Djauan Rainbow Serpent variant. Holds the life-sinews of every being; if she lets go, that being dies.

gods.name: Djanggawul
gods.description: Three Yolŋu ancestors — a brother and two sisters — who traveled from the sunrise island Burralku, creating the landscape and the Yolŋu people.

gods.name: Mimi
gods.description: Arnhem Land spirits — stick-thin, they live in rock crevices. Taught the ancestors how to hunt and paint.

gods.name: Tate
gods.description: Wind. Father of the Four Winds of the directions. His sons established the sacred directions of the Lakota world.

gods.name: Wohpe
gods.description: Falling-star goddess of peace and meditation. Gave the sacred pipe to the Lakota as White Buffalo Calf Woman.

gods.name: Hastseyalti
gods.description: 'Talking God'. Chief of the Diné yei (Holy People). Dawn-god of the east; teacher of the sacred ways.

gods.name: Gaoh
gods.description: Master of the four winds. Each direction is ruled by one of his animal servants — the bear, panther, moose, and fawn.

gods.name: Rod
gods.description: Primordial Slavic ancestor-god; source of being. Accompanied by the Rozhanitsy (birth-fates).

gods.name: Belobog
gods.description: 'White God' — hypothetical counterweight to Chernobog. Worship is debated — he may be a post-hoc pairing created by medieval chroniclers.

gods.name: Jah
gods.description: God of Rastafari. A form of 'Yah' (Yahweh) — the same God of Judaism and Christianity, understood to have been manifested in the person of Haile Selassie I.

gods.name: Haile Selassie I
gods.description: 1892–1975. Emperor of Ethiopia. Worshipped by Rastafari as Jah incarnate and the returned Messiah. Rejected the role in his lifetime; deposed in 1974.

gods.name: Bahá'u'lláh
gods.description: 1817–1892. Founder of the Bahá'í Faith. Held to be the latest in a series of 'Manifestations of God' including Moses, Buddha, Jesus, and Muhammad.

gods.name: The Báb
gods.description: 1819–1850. Persian merchant-prophet who declared himself the Gate (Báb) to the coming Manifestation. Executed by Qajar authorities. Herald of Bahá'u'lláh.

gods.name: Cao Đài
gods.description: 'Highest Palace' — the Supreme Being of Cao Đài. Represented by a single eye (the Divine Eye) rather than a figure.

gods.name: Diêu Trì Kim Mẫu
gods.description: Heavenly Mother; feminine principle of the divine in Cao Đài. Derived from the Chinese Queen Mother of the West.

gods.name: Nanshe
gods.description: Sumerian goddess of social justice, divination, and prophecy. Advocate of the poor and oppressed; interpreter of dreams. Her city was Nina in the marshlands, where her sacred fish-bird creatures were venerated.

gods.name: Chiron
gods.description: Wise centaur — son of Cronus (in horse-form) and the nymph Philyra. Tutor to Achilles, Jason, Heracles, and Asclepius. Immortal until an accidental Heraclean arrow drove him to give up his immortality to Prometheus. Placed in the sky as Sagittarius or Centaurus.

notable_mentions.name: Aegeus
notable_mentions.note: King of Athens. Threw himself into the sea named for him when he wrongly believed Theseus dead.

notable_mentions.name: Atalanta
notable_mentions.note: Swift huntress. Only woman on the Calydonian Boar hunt; outran every suitor until one dropped golden apples.

notable_mentions.name: Ganymede
notable_mentions.note: Trojan prince abducted by Zeus to serve as cupbearer of the gods. Immortalized as Aquarius.

notable_mentions.name: Narcissus
notable_mentions.note: Pined away staring at his own reflection. Became the flower that bears his name.

notable_mentions.name: Echo
notable_mentions.note: Nymph reduced to repeating others' words. Loved Narcissus from afar until only her voice remained.

notable_mentions.name: Pygmalion
notable_mentions.note: Sculptor whose ivory statue of Galatea was brought to life by Aphrodite.

notable_mentions.name: Midas
notable_mentions.note: Phrygian king who asked that all he touched turn to gold — and regretted it.

notable_mentions.name: Sarpedon
notable_mentions.note: Son of Zeus and Europa. King of Lycia; died at Troy.

notable_mentions.name: Meleager
notable_mentions.note: Leader of the Calydonian Boar hunt. His life was tied to a log his mother burned.

notable_mentions.name: Cadmus
notable_mentions.note: Covered in the main Cadmus entry — founder of Thebes.

notable_mentions.name: Jocasta
notable_mentions.note: Theban queen; Oedipus's mother-wife. Hanged herself on discovering the truth.

notable_mentions.name: Creon
notable_mentions.note: Theban regent. Forbade burial of Polyneices, setting Antigone's tragedy.

notable_mentions.name: Peleus
notable_mentions.note: Mortal husband of Thetis; father of Achilles.

notable_mentions.name: Rhadamanthus
notable_mentions.note: Brother of Minos; second judge of the underworld.

notable_mentions.name: Triptolemus
notable_mentions.note: Demeter's protégé. Taught humanity agriculture; rode a dragon-drawn chariot.

notable_mentions.name: Iris
notable_mentions.note: Catalogued separately — rainbow messenger of Hera.

notable_mentions.name: Flora
notable_mentions.note: Goddess of flowers and spring. Her Floralia festival was famously licentious.

notable_mentions.name: Pax
notable_mentions.note: Personified peace; cult of the Ara Pacis Augustae.

notable_mentions.name: Silvanus
notable_mentions.note: God of woods and uncultivated fields.

notable_mentions.name: Hygieia
notable_mentions.note: Greek/Roman goddess of health — daughter of Asclepius, catalogued in that entry.

notable_mentions.name: Vertumnus
notable_mentions.note: Shape-shifter god of seasons and plant growth. Wooed Pomona in disguise.

notable_mentions.name: Concordia
notable_mentions.note: Personified civic harmony.

notable_mentions.name: Victoria
notable_mentions.note: Roman Nike — goddess of victory.

notable_mentions.name: Anhur
notable_mentions.note: War-god of This and Lower Egypt; 'Skybearer'.

notable_mentions.name: Banebdjedet
notable_mentions.note: Ram-god of Mendes. Souls of Osiris were said to inhabit him.

notable_mentions.name: Kuk
notable_mentions.note: One of the Hermopolitan Ogdoad, personifying primeval darkness.

notable_mentions.name: Meskhenet
notable_mentions.note: Goddess of childbirth bricks — women squatted over her bricks to give birth.

notable_mentions.name: Nehebkau
notable_mentions.note: Two-headed serpent-guardian of the underworld.

notable_mentions.name: Pakhet
notable_mentions.note: Lion-goddess of the eastern desert; 'she who scratches'.

notable_mentions.name: Tatenen
notable_mentions.note: Primordial earth-god; the risen mound of creation at Memphis.

notable_mentions.name: Dellingr
notable_mentions.note: Personified dawn; father of Dagr (day).

notable_mentions.name: Gefn
notable_mentions.note: Alternate name for Freyja as 'giver'.

notable_mentions.name: Hlín
notable_mentions.note: Guardian-goddess attending Frigg; protects those Frigg wishes to save.

notable_mentions.name: Sága
notable_mentions.note: Goddess of histories; drinks daily with Odin from golden cups.

notable_mentions.name: Þjálfi and Röskva
notable_mentions.note: Thor's mortal servants — a brother and sister bonded to him after Thjalfi damaged one of his goats.

notable_mentions.name: Haya
notable_mentions.note: Grain-god; husband of Nisaba.

notable_mentions.name: Ishum
notable_mentions.note: Mild attendant of Nergal; fire-brother to Gibil.

notable_mentions.name: Nungal
notable_mentions.note: Goddess of prisons in Sumerian tradition.

notable_mentions.name: Ninisina
notable_mentions.note: Healing-goddess of Isin. Often merged with Gula.

notable_mentions.name: Mitra (Vedic)
notable_mentions.note: Vedic god of contracts; cognate with Iranian Mithra.

notable_mentions.name: Aryaman
notable_mentions.note: Aditya of hospitality and the milky way.

notable_mentions.name: Bhaga
notable_mentions.note: Aditya of wealth and shares.

notable_mentions.name: Shantanu
notable_mentions.note: Ancestor-king of the Kurus; father of Bhishma by Ganga.

notable_mentions.name: Duryodhana
notable_mentions.note: Eldest Kaurava prince; chief antagonist of the Mahabharata war.

notable_mentions.name: Karna (Mahabharata)
notable_mentions.note: Catalogued — son of Surya, tragic Kaurava warrior.

notable_mentions.name: Kunti
notable_mentions.note: Mother of the first three Pandavas and Karna.

notable_mentions.name: Gandhari
notable_mentions.note: Mother of the Kauravas; blindfolded herself for life to share her blind husband's world.

notable_mentions.name: Ao Guang
notable_mentions.note: Dragon King of the Eastern Sea.

notable_mentions.name: Bixia Yuanjun
notable_mentions.note: Goddess of Mount Tai, dawn, and childbirth.

notable_mentions.name: Cangjie
notable_mentions.note: Four-eyed official credited with inventing Chinese characters.

notable_mentions.name: Xuannu
notable_mentions.note: Catalogued as Jiuqian Xuannü above.

notable_mentions.name: Omoikane
notable_mentions.note: Kami of wisdom and counsel; advised the other kami during Amaterasu's hiding.

notable_mentions.name: Ōwatatsumi
notable_mentions.note: Dragon-king of the sea; father of Toyotama-hime.

notable_mentions.name: Sarutahiko
notable_mentions.note: Kami who guided Ninigi's descent to earth.

notable_mentions.name: Belisama
notable_mentions.note: Gallic goddess of fire and forge; consort of Belenus.

notable_mentions.name: Lenus
notable_mentions.note: Treveran healer-god; equated with Mars.

notable_mentions.name: Lugus
notable_mentions.note: Gallic continental form of Lugh; triple-aspected.

notable_mentions.name: Epona
notable_mentions.note: Catalogued — Gallic horse-goddess adopted by Roman cavalry.

notable_mentions.name: Jarilo (alt)
notable_mentions.note: Catalogued as Yarilo.

notable_mentions.name: Rusalki
notable_mentions.note: Water-nymphs — spirits of drowned women.

notable_mentions.name: Centeotl
notable_mentions.note: Catalogued — god of maize.

notable_mentions.name: Teteo Innan
notable_mentions.note: 'Mother of the Gods' — earth aspect of Coatlicue.

notable_mentions.name: Tlazolteotl
notable_mentions.note: Catalogued — 'filth-eater' of confession and purification.

notable_mentions.name: Orula
notable_mentions.note: Catalogued as Orunmila.

notable_mentions.name: Nana Buluku
notable_mentions.note: Catalogued — primordial grandmother of Mawu and Lisa.

notable_mentions.name: Laka
notable_mentions.note: Hawaiian goddess of hula; younger sister of Pele.

notable_mentions.name: Kamapua'a
notable_mentions.note: Hawaiian pig-man demigod; lover of Pele.

notable_mentions.name: Pariacaca
notable_mentions.note: Pre-Inca mountain-falcon god of Huarochirí.

notable_mentions.name: Shekhinah
notable_mentions.note: Catalogued as Malkhut/Shekhinah in the Kabbalistic tree; the indwelling divine presence in Judaism generally.

notable_mentions.name: Prometheus (mentioned)
notable_mentions.note: Fully catalogued.

notable_mentions.name: Wotan
notable_mentions.note: Continental Germanic form of Odin; known via Tacitus's Mercury.

notable_mentions.name: Donar
notable_mentions.note: Continental Germanic form of Thor.

notable_mentions.name: Saxnōt
notable_mentions.note: Continental Saxon god named alongside Wotan and Donar in Christianization oaths.

notable_mentions.name: Nerthus
notable_mentions.note: Catalogued — Germanic earth-goddess described by Tacitus.

notable_mentions.name: Vayu (as Dikpala)
notable_mentions.note: Wind-god Vayu also serves as Dikpala of the northwest — see his main entry.

notable_mentions.name: Anahit-Tir-Mihr Triad
notable_mentions.note: Armenian late-pagan devotional triad venerated together; each figure is catalogued.
